ideas.title: SM-Partitioned T_gen/T_merge Decoupling
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's Eq.5 feasibility constraint to GPU by treating shared memory as M and HBM as D, partitioning SMs between run generation and merge phases.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:SorterCore::run_generation_internal (line 292) + multi_merge_internal (line 309)
ideas.crocsort_concept: Eq.5 single-step feasibility: T_gen * T_merge <= E*rho^2*M^2/(D*P); phase asymmetry T_gen > T_merge
ideas.gpu_primitive: cooperative_groups (cg::grid_group), cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialBlockSize, shared memory as run-gen workspace
ideas.detail: Each SM runs replacement selection in shared memory (228KB on H100), generating sorted runs that spill to HBM. The SM count split between run-gen and merge kernels mirrors CrocSort's thread pool split. Cooperative groups enable grid-wide synchronization between phases without kernel relaunch. The feasibility equation T_gen*T_merge <= E*rho^2*SMEM^2/(HBM*128B) determines the optimal SM split.
ideas.novelty: CUB DeviceMergeSort and Thrust sort_by_key treat sort as a monolithic operation. No existing GPU sort framework applies an analytical model to partition compute resources between run generation and merge. ModernGPU's mergesort is purely in-HBM with no replacement selection.
related_work: Stehle & Jacobsen, GPU-Accelerated External Sorting, SIGMOD 2017
Chen et al., PARADIS: Partitioning and Replicating Data in a Sort, VLDB 2015
ideas.experiment: Compare SM-partitioned sort vs CUB DeviceMergeSort on A100 80GB with datasets 1-200GB, measuring total sort time and HBM bandwidth utilization at different SM split ratios.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs Stehle 2017: they treat GPU sort as sequential kernel launches with no analytical model for phase balance. Gap vs PARADIS: CPU-only, no GPU memory hierarchy mapping. Risk: shared memory per SM is only 48-228KB, limiting run length to ~500-2000 records; need cp.async pipelining. Bank conflicts during tree operations (32 banks, 4B stride) could cause 2-32x slowdown. Eq.5 GPU analogue: M=SMEM_per_SM (228KB), D=HBM_data (80GB), P=cache_line (128B), T_gen=runs_in_SMEM, T_merge=fanin_from_HBM, rho=0.7 (SMEM utilization with tree overhead), E=2.
ideas.experiment_plan: Baseline: CUB DeviceMergeSort, Thrust sort_by_key, ModernGPU mergesort. Metrics: (1) end-to-end sort throughput in GB/s, (2) SM utilization % from NSight Compute section-level analysis, (3) HBM bandwidth utilization % (achieved vs peak). Datasets: GenSort 80GB (fills A100 HBM), lineitem-SF100 (~24GB), FreqKey 40GB (uniform payload), HeavyKey 40GB (256x skew). Milestones: M1 — smem_replacement_selection_kernel generating correct runs; M2 — cooperative_groups phase transition working; M3 — SM split ratio sweep finding optimal T_gen/T_merge. Validation: verify that Eq.5 with GPU parameters predicts the optimal SM split ratio within 10%. Expected: optimal split allocates ~60% SMs to run-gen (compute-bound) and ~40% to merge (bandwidth-bound), achieving 1.5-2x over CUB for datasets exceeding HBM.

ideas.title: M/D Ratio Governs GPU Sort Performance Surface Shape
ideas.summary: CrocSort's cloud finding that M/D ratio determines performance surface shape predicts that GPU's extremely low SMEM/HBM ratio (~0.003%) creates a sharper optimal ridge requiring precise configuration.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:SorterCore (line 218) — run_gen_mem, merge_threads, merge_fanin config space
ideas.crocsort_concept: M/D ratio governs performance surface; top-3 configs transfer across scales; config gap exceeds 7x
ideas.gpu_primitive: cudaDeviceGetAttribute for smem/HBM sizing, cudaFuncSetAttribute for dynamic shared memory
ideas.detail: With SMEM/HBM ratio of ~0.003% per SM (vs CPU M/D of ~1-10%), the GPU performance surface should be extremely sharp — small config changes cause large performance swings. This predicts that GPU sort tuning is even more critical than CPU. The scale transfer property (top-3 configs at SF50 stay within 5% at SF200) should also hold for GPU across HBM sizes.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort study has characterized the performance surface as a function of memory hierarchy ratios. CUB and Thrust use fixed heuristics without modeling the config space.
related_work: Satish et al., Designing Efficient Sorting Algorithms for Manycore GPUs, IPDPS 2009
Merrill & Grimshaw, High Performance and Scalable Radix Sorting, 2011
ideas.experiment: Sweep GPU sort config space (block size, smem allocation, merge fanin) across A100/H100/MI250X and measure performance surface shape.

ideas.title: Huffman Merge Schedule as Warp-Level Persistent Merge Tree
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's Huffman merge schedule (minimizing L_avg) to a persistent GPU kernel where warps form a merge tree, using __shfl_sync for intra-warp key exchange.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:multi_merge_with_hooks — recursive merge with fanin control; src/sort/ovc/merge.rs:ZeroCopyMergeWithOVC
ideas.crocsort_concept: Total I/O = 2D*(1 + L_avg); Huffman schedule minimizes L_avg; multi-level merge in engine.rs
ideas.gpu_primitive: __shfl_sync, cooperative_groups, persistent kernel (grid-level loop)
ideas.detail: Instead of launching separate kernels per merge level (each incurring HBM read/write), a persistent kernel keeps the entire Huffman merge tree resident. Each warp implements a 32-wide merge network using shuffle instructions for key exchange. Intermediate results stay in registers/shared memory between tree levels, eliminating HBM round-trips that dominate multi-level merge. The ZeroCopyMergeWithOVC pattern (keys in tree, values streamed) maps to keys in registers + values in HBM with cp.async.
ideas.novelty: ModernGPU and CUB implement flat merge with separate kernel launches per level. No existing GPU merge uses a Huffman schedule or persistent kernel to minimize total memory traffic across levels.
related_work: Green et al., Merge Path - A Visually Intuitive Approach to Parallel Merging, 2012
Baxter, ModernGPU: Merge and Segmented Sort, 2013
ideas.experiment: Compare persistent Huffman merge vs CUB multi-pass merge on 80GB dataset, measuring total HBM traffic via NSight Compute L2 counters.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs Merge Path (2012): only 2-way merge, no multi-level scheduling. Gap vs ModernGPU: recursive 2-way merge with separate kernels per level, no Huffman awareness. Risk: warp divergence in loser tree tournament — pass() in tree_of_losers.rs:156 has variable-length Phase 1/2 loops that map poorly to SIMD. If 32 threads take different paths, effective throughput drops to 1/32. Mitigation: use bitonic merge network instead of loser tree at warp level. Eq.5 analogue: L_avg -> number of persistent kernel iterations, D -> HBM traffic per level, M -> L2 cache working set (~40MB), P -> 128B cache line, T_merge -> fanin per warp.

ideas.title: Two-Sided Memory Threshold as L1/L2 GPU Crossover
ideas.summary: CrocSort's two-sided memory threshold (merge-bound below, comparison-bound above) maps to GPU L1 vs L2 cache boundary.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:merge_memory_budget_bytes (line 53)
ideas.crocsort_concept: Two-sided memory threshold; below = merge-bound, above = run-gen comparison-bound
ideas.gpu_primitive: cudaFuncSetCacheConfig, NSight Compute cache profiling
ideas.detail: On GPU, L1 (shared memory, ~100KB) represents the low-memory regime and L2 (~40MB) the high-memory regime. When merge working set fits in L2, the sort is compute-bound. When it doesn't, it's bandwidth-bound. The threshold determines optimal merge fanin.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort characterizes the L1/L2 crossover as a two-sided threshold analogous to CrocSort's CPU analysis.
related_work: Satish et al., IPDPS 2009
Ye et al., IPDPS 2019
ideas.experiment: Profile GPU merge with varying fanin (2-256) on A100, measuring L2 hit rate to identify crossover.

ideas.title: Phase Asymmetry as Kernel Occupancy Balancing
ideas.summary: CrocSort's optimal T_gen > T_merge maps to GPU kernel occupancy tuning between run-gen and merge phases.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:SorterCore run_gen_threads vs merge_threads (line 221-223)
ideas.crocsort_concept: Phase asymmetry: T_gen > T_merge optimal
ideas.gpu_primitive: cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialBlockSize
ideas.detail: Run generation needs compute (high occupancy), merge needs bandwidth (fewer warps, larger buffers). The asymmetric occupancy matches CrocSort's phase imbalance.
ideas.novelty: Existing GPU sorts use uniform kernel configs. Analytical asymmetry-based occupancy tuning is novel.
related_work: Volkov, PhD Thesis 2016
Stehle & Jacobsen, SIGMOD 2017
ideas.experiment: Sweep occupancy configs for run-gen vs merge and measure total sort time on GenSort 80GB.

ideas.title: Symmetric Boundary T* as GPU Multi-SM Run Pooling
ideas.summary: CrocSort's T* = rho*M*sqrt(E)/sqrt(D*P) yields T*~0.07 for a single SM, proving that GPU external sort requires cross-SM run pooling to generate enough runs.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:execute_run_generation (line 59) — parallel thread pooling of scanners
ideas.crocsort_concept: Symmetric boundary T* = rho*M*sqrt(E)/sqrt(D*P); T*=20.5 for CPU parameters
ideas.gpu_primitive: cooperative_groups::grid_group for cross-SM synchronization, atomicAdd for global run counter
ideas.detail: Computing T* with GPU parameters (M=228KB, D=80GB, P=128B, rho=0.7, E=2): T* = 0.7*228KB*1.41/sqrt(80GB*128B) = ~0.07. This means a single SM cannot even produce one full run — unlike CPU where T*=20.5. The fix: pool shared memory across 128+ SMs, creating a virtual large M. Each SM contributes sorted chunks via atomic append to a global run buffer in HBM. This requires cooperative groups grid-level sync.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort computes the symmetric boundary equation. The realization that T*<<1 per SM is a novel CrocSort-specific insight that reshapes GPU sort architecture.
related_work: Stehle & Jacobsen, SIGMOD 2017
Dakkak et al., Accelerating Reduction and Scan Using Tensor Core Units, ICS 2019
ideas.experiment: Measure actual run count and expansion factor E when pooling 1, 16, 64, 128 SMs for run generation on A100 with GenSort 80GB.

ideas.title: Storage Latency Sensitivity on Multi-GPU NVLink Topology
ideas.summary: CrocSort's finding that 100us latency widens config gap 2.5x predicts that multi-GPU sort over PCIe (1-10us) vs NVLink (0.5us) will show dramatic performance surface shifts.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:SorterCore config space; cloud paper storage-aware I/O term
ideas.crocsort_concept: 100us storage latency widens gap 2.5x->3.0x; at 500us optimal shifts to fewer threads
ideas.gpu_primitive: cudaMemcpyPeerAsync for NVLink transfers, NCCL for multi-GPU communication
ideas.detail: CrocSort found that adding a storage-aware I/O term cuts prediction error from 60% to 12%. For multi-GPU sort, the analogous term models NVLink/PCIe latency. PCIe latency (~5us) vs NVLink (~0.5us) is a 10x gap, which by CrocSort's findings should produce measurably different optimal configurations.
ideas.novelty: No multi-GPU sort study uses CrocSort's latency-sensitivity analysis to predict configuration shifts across interconnect types.
related_work: Tanasic et al., GPU Multi-GPU Sorting, PPoPP 2014
Rui et al., Efficient Multi-GPU Sort, TPDS 2021
ideas.experiment: Run GPU sort across 1-8 GPUs on NVLink vs PCIe topologies, measuring performance surface shape changes.

ideas.title: Budget Split L* for Dynamic Shared Memory Carveout
ideas.summary: CrocSort's L* = X*R/(R+2S) formula for tree/buffer split maps to GPU dynamic shared memory carveout between loser tree and record buffer.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/replacement_selection/memory.rs:MemoryManager::with_limit (line 122) — best-fit allocator with limit; tol_mm.rs:ReplacementSelectionMM tree+buffer split
ideas.crocsort_concept: L* = X*R/(R+2S); starts at 50% cap, raises to L* once avg record size measured
ideas.gpu_primitive: cudaFuncSetAttribute(cudaFuncAttributeMaxDynamicSharedMemorySize), two-phase kernel launch
ideas.detail: Phase 1 kernel uses 50% smem for tree, 50% for records (CrocSort's initial cap). After measuring avg record size R via atomic accumulation, Phase 2 kernel reconfigures smem split to L* = SMEM*R/(R+2*sizeof(SmemLoserNode)). The MemoryManager's best-fit strategy (64KB extents, 32B classes) maps to a simplified smem sub-allocator with warp-aligned blocks.
ideas.novelty: GPU sorts use fixed smem layouts. Dynamic carveout based on runtime record statistics, derived from CrocSort's analytical formula, is entirely novel.
related_work: Li et al., Adaptive Shared Memory Configuration on GPUs, ISCA 2020
Peters et al., Register and Shared Memory for GPU Sort, Euro-Par 2019
ideas.experiment: Compare fixed vs adaptive smem carveout on datasets with varying record sizes (10B-10KB), measuring run length and throughput.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs Li 2020: workload-agnostic smem partitioning without analytical formula. Gap vs Peters 2019: static allocation, no runtime adaptation. Risk: cudaFuncSetAttribute is per-kernel, requiring kernel relaunch between phases. Two-phase approach adds ~10us overhead. Alternative: use a single kernel with compile-time templates for common record size ranges. Eq.5 analogue: L* maps directly — X=total_smem, R=avg_record_bytes (measured at runtime), S=sizeof(SmemLoserNode)=8B. The 50% initial cap and progressive raise mirrors MemoryManager behavior exactly.

ideas.title: 5% L2 Residency Control for Sparse Index Pages
ideas.summary: CrocSort's 5% memory budget for sparse indexes maps to GPU L2 persistence API, pinning merge partition metadata in L2 cache for fast boundary lookups.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:INDEX_BUDGET_PCT=5 (line 50); src/sort/core/sparse_index.rs:SparseIndex (4KB pages, handle encoding)
ideas.crocsort_concept: 5% reserved for sparse indexes; SparseIndexPagePool for page recycling; dynamic stride for budget control
ideas.gpu_primitive: cudaStreamAttrValue::accessPolicyWindow (L2 persistence API), cudaAccessPropertyPersisting
ideas.detail: Pin 5% of L2 (~2MB on A100) for sparse index pages. CrocSort's 4KB page format transfers directly — each GPU page holds serialized (file_offset, key_len, key) entries with handle encoding (20-bit page_idx, 12-bit byte_offset). During merge boundary search, all sparse index reads hit L2 instead of HBM, reducing boundary computation latency by ~10x. The SparseIndexPagePool concept maps to L2 persistence window management.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort uses L2 persistence for partition metadata. CrocSort's 5% budget concept applied to GPU L2 persistence is a novel cross-layer optimization combining sort algorithm design with cache hardware control.
related_work: Jia et al., Dissecting NVIDIA Ampere via Microbenchmarking, 2021
Ashkiani et al., Efficient GPU Merge Path with Partitioning, PPoPP 2022
ideas.experiment: Measure merge boundary search latency with and without L2 persistence on A100, varying number of runs (10-10000) and sparse index density.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs Jia 2021: characterizes L2 persistence but not for sort. Gap vs Ashkiani 2022: uses partitioning but no L2 residency control. Risk: L2 persistence window is limited; with many runs, index may exceed budget. Ampere L2 persistence can only pin ~30% of L2 effectively. Also, persistence reduces effective cache for run data. CrocSort's budget_pages() (sparse_index.rs:261) already handles this tradeoff. Eq.5 analogue: 5% budget = INDEX_BUDGET_PCT * merge_memory_bytes / 100. On GPU: 0.05 * L2_size = 0.05 * 40MB = 2MB. Each 4KB page = ~500 pages pinnable.
ideas.experiment_plan: Baseline: GPU merge with standard L2 caching (no persistence), CUB DeviceMergeSort (no partition metadata). Metrics: (1) merge boundary search latency in microseconds, (2) L2 hit rate % for sparse index accesses (NSight Compute l2_hit_rate), (3) overall merge throughput in GB/s. Datasets: GenSort 80GB with varying run counts (10, 100, 1000, 10000 runs), lineitem-SF100 with sparse indexes at stride 100 and 1000. Milestones: M1 — sparse_index_to_gpu_transfer kernel; M2 — L2 persistence configuration validated via NSight; M3 — full merge with L2-pinned boundary search. Validation: verify that boundary search latency drops proportionally to L2 hit rate improvement. CrocSort's read amplification metric (lib.rs:76) should show minimal overhead from sparse index. Expected: L2 persistence yields 5-10x faster boundary search, translating to 10-20% overall merge speedup when run count > 100.

ideas.title: Expansion Factor E=2 Under GPU Register Pressure
ideas.summary: CrocSort's replacement selection expansion factor E=2 may degrade on GPU if loser tree state spills from registers to local memory (HBM), reducing effective workspace.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/replacement_selection/tol_mm.rs:ReplacementSelectionMM — tree + workspace in MemoryManager
ideas.crocsort_concept: Expansion factor E=2 on random input; longer runs on presorted data
ideas.gpu_primitive: Register file (255 registers per thread on CUDA), --maxrregcount compiler flag
ideas.detail: Replacement selection requires maintaining loser tree state across iterations. On GPU, each thread has 255 registers. A loser tree with capacity C needs ~2*C*sizeof(node) bytes of state. If C=64, that's 512B = 128 registers, leaving only 127 for other variables. Register spill to local memory (HBM round-trip) degrades throughput and may reduce effective E below 2.
ideas.novelty: No study has measured how GPU register pressure affects replacement selection expansion factor.
related_work: Volkov, Better Performance at Lower Occupancy, GTC 2010
Chhugani et al., Efficient Sort on AVX, VLDB 2008
ideas.experiment: Measure expansion factor E with varying --maxrregcount settings (32, 64, 128, 255) on GPU replacement selection.

ideas.title: Run Count Prediction via GPU-Adapted Eq.5 with HBM Bandwidth Term
ideas.summary: Extend CrocSort's Eq.5 with a GPU-specific HBM bandwidth term B_hbm to predict optimal run count, accounting for the fact that GPU merge is bandwidth-limited not latency-limited.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:SorterCore::multi_merge_internal (line 309); cloud paper: storage-aware I/O term
ideas.crocsort_concept: Storage-aware I/O term cuts prediction error 60%->12%; total I/O = 2D*(1+L_avg)
ideas.gpu_primitive: HBM2e bandwidth measurement via cudaMemcpy benchmarking, NSight Compute DRAM throughput
ideas.detail: CrocSort's cloud paper adds a storage latency term to improve prediction. For GPU, add a bandwidth term: T_sort = T_gen(compute) + 2*D*(1+L_avg)/B_hbm. With B_hbm=2TB/s on A100, merge of 80GB takes ~80ms at L_avg=0. Each additional merge level adds 80ms. This predicts the break-even point between more runs (shorter run-gen) vs fewer merge levels.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort cost model combines CrocSort's I/O model with measured HBM bandwidth to predict optimal configuration.
related_work: Stehle & Jacobsen, SIGMOD 2017
Bandle et al., To Partition or Not to Partition, VLDB 2021
ideas.experiment: Validate GPU-adapted Eq.5 predictions against measured sort times across run counts (5-1000) and data sizes (1-80GB) on A100.

ideas.title: Presorted Input Detection via GPU Streaming Histogram
ideas.summary: CrocSort's E>2 on presorted data motivates a GPU streaming sortedness detector that adjusts run generation strategy dynamically.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/replacement_selection/heap.rs:should_defer_to_next_run (line 123) — detects out-of-order records
ideas.crocsort_concept: Expansion factor E=2 on random, E>>2 on presorted; replacement selection exploits existing order
ideas.gpu_primitive: warp ballot (__ballot_sync) for parallel sortedness detection, atomic histogram
ideas.detail: Each warp loads 32 consecutive records and uses __ballot_sync to count how many pairs (r[i], r[i+1]) are already sorted. The resulting per-warp bitmask is atomically accumulated into a global sortedness histogram. If sortedness > threshold, the kernel switches from radix sort to replacement selection (which benefits from pre-existing order, achieving E>>2). This mirrors CrocSort's should_defer_to_next_run check but at warp scale.
ideas.novelty: GPU sorts (CUB, Thrust) always use the same algorithm regardless of input order. Adaptive algorithm selection based on streaming sortedness measurement is novel on GPU.
related_work: Estivill-Castro & Wood, A Survey of Adaptive Sorting Algorithms, ACM Computing Surveys 1992
Deshpande & Narang, Adaptive GPU Sorting, GPGPU-6 2013
ideas.experiment: Compare adaptive vs fixed sort on GenSort (random), lineitem (partially sorted), and fully sorted inputs, measuring throughput and run count.

ideas.title: Imbalance Factor r as GPU Thread Block Load Balancing
ideas.summary: CrocSort's imbalance_factor for first-partition heaviness maps to GPU thread block assignment for merge, where the first block gets disproportionate work to handle sorted-input skew.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:SorterCore::imbalance_factor (line 258); docs/merge_partitioning_with_sparse_indexes.md: heavy/each formula
ideas.crocsort_concept: Boundary vector: heavy = min(max(r,1)/N, 1); each = (1-heavy)/(N-1); imbalance handling
ideas.gpu_primitive: Dynamic parallelism (cudaLaunchKernel from device), load-balanced work assignment
ideas.detail: CrocSort's partition formula heavy = min(max(r,1)/N, 1) creates unequal partition sizes. On GPU, this maps to assigning more thread blocks to heavy partitions. Instead of uniform grid launch, use dynamic parallelism: the first kernel computes boundaries, then launches child kernels with grid sizes proportional to partition weight. This handles the HeavyKey case where 256x payload skew causes extreme partition imbalance.
ideas.novelty: GPU merge sorts use uniform partition sizes (Merge Path). CrocSort's analytical imbalance factor applied to dynamic GPU work assignment is novel.
related_work: Green et al., Merge Path, 2012
Wassenberg & Sanders, Engineering a Multi-core Radix Sort, Euro-Par 2011
ideas.experiment: Measure merge thread time imbalance (max/avg ratio from MergeStats) on GPU with HeavyKey dataset under uniform vs CrocSort-weighted partition.

ideas.title: Read Amplification Tracking as GPU Performance Counter Metric
ideas.summary: CrocSort's read amplification metric (merge reads / run-gen writes) maps to a GPU HBM traffic ratio measurable via hardware counters.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/lib.rs:SortStats display (line 68-107) — read_amplification = merge_read_bytes / run_gen_write_bytes
ideas.crocsort_concept: Read amplification from sparse indexing overhead; excess reads as efficiency metric
ideas.gpu_primitive: NSight Compute DRAM read/write counters (dram__bytes_read, dram__bytes_write)
ideas.detail: CrocSort tracks read amplification as a key metric. On GPU, the same ratio (merge HBM reads / run-gen HBM writes) can be computed via NSight Compute counters between kernel launches. A read amplification of 1.0x means perfect efficiency; >1.0x indicates sparse index overhead or suboptimal partitioning. This gives a hardware-grounded optimization target for GPU sort development.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort framework tracks read amplification as an explicit optimization metric tied to the sort algorithm's data flow.
related_work: Athanassoulis et al., Designing Access Methods, VLDB 2016
Idreos et al., Database Cracking, CIDR 2007
ideas.experiment: Measure GPU read amplification across run counts and merge strategies, correlating with CrocSort's CPU measurements.

ideas.title: GPU Merge Cost Model with L_avg Predicting Kernel Launch Count
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's L_avg (average merge depth per byte under Huffman schedule) to predict the number of GPU merge kernel launches, minimizing total HBM traffic.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:multi_merge_with_hooks — multi-level merge; lib.rs:per_merge_stats tracking multiple passes
ideas.crocsort_concept: Total I/O = 2D*(1+L_avg); L_avg under Huffman schedule; multi-level merge stats
ideas.gpu_primitive: cudaStreamWaitEvent for kernel chaining, cudaGraph for multi-kernel optimization
ideas.detail: Each merge kernel launch reads and writes data to HBM. Total HBM traffic = 2*D*(1+L_avg) where L_avg depends on the merge schedule. By computing the optimal Huffman schedule before launching kernels, we can use cudaGraph to capture and optimize the entire merge DAG, reducing launch overhead and enabling HBM traffic prediction. CrocSort's per_merge_stats (lib.rs:296) provides the per-level accounting needed to validate the model.
ideas.novelty: GPU merge implementations use fixed schedules. Using CrocSort's Huffman schedule analysis to predict and minimize total HBM traffic via kernel graph optimization is novel.
related_work: Baxter, ModernGPU Merge, 2013
NVIDIA, CUDA Graphs Programming Guide, 2023
ideas.experiment: Compare total HBM traffic (measured via NSight) between fixed 2-way merge, fixed k-way merge, and Huffman-scheduled merge across 80GB dataset with varying run counts.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs ModernGPU: uses fixed 2-way merge schedule, no Huffman optimization. Gap vs CUDA Graphs: captures arbitrary kernel DAGs but no sort-specific scheduling. Risk: kernel graph capture requires deterministic execution; dynamic merge decisions (like CrocSort's multi_merge choosing fanin per level) must be resolved before graph capture. L2 cache state between kernels in a graph may not persist. Eq.5 analogue: L_avg = sum(level_i * bytes_at_level_i) / D, where each level is a kernel launch. M = L2 cache (determines fanin per level), P = 128B cache line.
ideas.experiment_plan: Baseline: CUB DeviceMergeSort (single-pass), ModernGPU multi-pass merge, hand-tuned k-way merge kernel. Metrics: (1) total HBM traffic in GB (dram__bytes_read + dram__bytes_write from NSight), (2) L_avg computed from per-kernel traffic, (3) end-to-end merge time in ms. Datasets: GenSort 80GB (A100 full HBM), lineitem-SF100 with 50/200/1000 runs. Milestones: M1 — Huffman schedule computation on host; M2 — cudaGraph capture of multi-kernel merge; M3 — L_avg measurement matching prediction. Validation: measured L_avg should match CrocSort's analytical prediction within 5%. Expected: Huffman schedule reduces total HBM traffic by 15-30% vs fixed k-way merge when run count > fanin, translating to proportional speedup.

ideas.title: Warp-Level OVC: 32-bit OVC in Registers for Comparison Shortcut
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's OVCU32 encoding (3-bit flag + 13-bit offset + 16-bit value) to GPU registers, enabling single-instruction OVC comparison via integer compare.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/ovc/offset_value_coding_32.rs:OVCU32 (line 100-148) — packed 32-bit OVC with flag/arity_minus_offset/value fields
ideas.crocsort_concept: OVC: first_differing_byte_offset + 2-byte value at that position; comparison shortcut; prefix truncation
ideas.gpu_primitive: 32-bit register comparison (single PTX instruction), __shfl_sync for OVC propagation within warp
ideas.detail: CrocSort's OVCU32 packs flag(3b)+arity_minus_offset(13b)+value(16b) into 32 bits, enabling comparison via a single integer compare (line 101: #[repr(transparent)] struct OVCU32(u32)). This maps perfectly to GPU 32-bit registers — each thread holds one OVC in a register, and comparison is a single PTX setp instruction. Within a warp, __shfl_sync propagates OVC values for the loser tree tournament without shared memory access. The compare_and_update method (tree_of_losers_ovc.rs) becomes a register-only operation.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort uses OVC encoding. GPU comparisons always compare full keys. Register-resident OVC enabling single-instruction comparison shortcut is entirely novel on GPU.
related_work: Graefe, Implementing Sorting in Database Systems, ACM Computing Surveys 2006
Muller et al., Cache-Efficient Aggregation, VLDB 2015
ideas.experiment: Compare merge throughput with OVC-enhanced comparison vs full key comparison on GPU, varying key length (10B-1000B) and prefix redundancy.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs Graefe 2006: CPU-focused OVC analysis, no GPU mapping. Gap vs Muller 2015: cache-efficient key comparison but no OVC encoding. Risk: OVC benefit depends on prefix redundancy — GenSort (low redundancy) shows negligible OVC benefit on CPU (CrocSort experimental result). For GPU, the benefit is amplified because key comparison requires HBM reads while OVC comparison uses only registers. Bank conflicts: OVC is 32 bits, perfectly aligned with register file. No conflicts. Eq.5 analogue: OVC reduces effective comparison cost — each OVC compare is 1 instruction vs O(key_len) for full compare. On GPU with 100B keys, this is ~25x fewer instructions, directly reducing the compute-bound phase duration.
ideas.experiment_plan: Baseline: CUB DeviceMergeSort (full key compare), ModernGPU merge (full key compare), custom GPU merge with memcmp. Metrics: (1) effective merge throughput in GB/s, (2) instructions per comparison (NSight Compute inst_executed), (3) HBM read bytes saved from prefix truncation. Datasets: GenSort 80GB (low prefix redundancy), lineitem-SF100 (high prefix redundancy — orderkey/linenumber prefix), FreqKey 40GB (moderate redundancy). Milestones: M1 — OVC encoding/decoding kernel (matches OVCU32::new/offset/value); M2 — warp-level OVC loser tree merge; M3 — full end-to-end sort with OVC. Validation: OVC should show negligible improvement on GenSort but ~50% time reduction on lineitem at high thread counts, matching CrocSort's CPU result. Expected: 2-5x merge throughput improvement on high-redundancy datasets due to register-only comparisons eliminating HBM key reads.

ideas.title: OVC Duplicate Shortcut as Warp-Level Fast Path
ideas.summary: CrocSort's DuplicateValue OVC flag (ovc.is_duplicate_value()) enables skipping the loser tree push for consecutive duplicate keys, mapping to a warp-level fast path on GPU.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/ovc/merge.rs:MergeWithOVC::next (line 42-47) — duplicate OVC shortcut: replace_top_ovc instead of tree.push
ideas.crocsort_concept: DuplicateValue flag; replace_top_ovc for O(1) duplicate handling; consecutive duplicates bypass tournament
ideas.gpu_primitive: __ballot_sync for detecting duplicate runs, warp-level predication
ideas.detail: In CrocSort's OVC merge (merge.rs:42-47), when next_item.ovc().is_duplicate_value(), the merge bypasses the full loser tree push and instead does replace_top_ovc — an O(1) operation. On GPU, a warp can use __ballot_sync to detect which threads have duplicate OVC flags, and those threads skip the tree tournament entirely. This creates a fast path where runs of duplicates are processed at memory bandwidth speed rather than comparison speed.
ideas.novelty: No GPU merge has a fast path for duplicate keys. CrocSort's O(1) duplicate handling via OVC flag is novel and maps to GPU warp-level predication.
related_work: Graefe, Implementing Sorting in Database Systems, 2006
Kim et al., Sort vs Hash Revisited, VLDB 2009
ideas.experiment: Measure merge throughput on FreqKey (many duplicates) vs GenSort (no duplicates) with and without duplicate fast path.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs Graefe: no GPU mapping of duplicate shortcut. Gap vs Kim: hash-based duplicate handling, not comparison-based. Risk: warp divergence — if some threads in a warp have duplicates and others don't, the fast path and slow path execute serially. Mitigation: sort records within each warp by source_idx so consecutive duplicates cluster on adjacent threads. Eq.5 analogue: duplicate shortcut reduces effective comparison count. For datasets with D% duplicates, the merge cost drops by D% * (full_compare_cost - 1).

ideas.title: OVC-Aware HBM Prefix Truncation for Merge I/O Reduction
ideas.summary: CrocSort's OVC enables prefix truncation (only writing the differing suffix to intermediate runs), reducing HBM write volume during merge by up to 50%.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/ovc/run.rs:RunWithOVC::append — writes OVC + key to run file; src/ovc/offset_value_coding_32.rs:OVCU32::offset() returns first differing byte
ideas.crocsort_concept: OVC enables prefix truncation for reduced intermediate I/O; offset field indicates shared prefix length
ideas.gpu_primitive: cp.async (asynchronous memory copy) for variable-length HBM writes, warp-level write coalescing
ideas.detail: When writing merge output to HBM, instead of writing full keys, write (OVC_u32 + key_suffix_from_offset). The OVC's offset field tells exactly how many prefix bytes are shared with the previous key. On high-redundancy data (lineitem), typical offset is 6-8 bytes on 20-byte keys, saving 30-40% of key write bandwidth. Use cp.async for variable-length writes to avoid warp stalls on uncoalesced HBM access.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort implements prefix truncation during merge output. CrocSort's OVC-based truncation applied to HBM write reduction is novel.
related_work: Bohm et al., Efficient Compression-Aware Sorting, SIGMOD 2011
Zukowski et al., Super-Scalar RAM-CPU Cache Compression, ICDE 2006
ideas.experiment: Measure HBM write bytes with and without prefix truncation on lineitem (high redundancy) and GenSort (low redundancy).

ideas.title: Register-File OVC Storage for Zero-Latency Comparison
ideas.summary: Store the entire OVC loser tree in GPU registers (not shared memory), achieving zero-latency comparisons for small fanin merge.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/ovc/tree_of_losers_ovc.rs:LoserTreeOVC — nodes store OVC + key reference; compare_and_update uses OVC for shortcut
ideas.crocsort_concept: LoserTreeOVC stores OVC per node; compare_and_update first checks OVC, then falls back to key comparison
ideas.gpu_primitive: Register file (255 registers per thread), indexed register access via PTX
ideas.detail: For merge fanin K<=16, the loser tree has 16 nodes of 4 bytes each (OVC only) = 64 bytes = 16 registers. One thread holds the entire tree in registers. Each comparison is a register-register operation (1 cycle latency vs 20+ cycles for shared memory). The OVC's 32-bit packed format means the tree state fits entirely in the register file. Fallback to full key comparison only happens when OVC values are equal (rare for well-distributed data).
ideas.novelty: GPU merges always use shared memory for merge state. A register-resident loser tree exploiting OVC's compact format is novel.
related_work: Graefe, Sorting & Indexing with Partitioned B-Trees, CIDR 2003
Balkesen et al., Multi-Core Main-Memory Joins, VLDB 2013
ideas.experiment: Compare register-OVC tree vs shared-memory tree for K=4,8,16,32 way merge, measuring cycles per comparison.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs Graefe: CPU register optimization, not GPU. Gap vs Balkesen: main-memory join with cache-conscious data structures, not register-resident. Risk: for K>16, register pressure becomes severe — 16 OVC nodes + 16 source indices + loop variables = ~50 registers, leaving ~200 for other work. At K=32, it's ~100 registers for tree alone, reducing occupancy. Mitigation: use K=8 or K=16 as sweet spot. Eq.5 analogue: register-OVC comparison cost = 1 cycle. Full key comparison in shared memory = ~20 cycles. For K-way merge with N records: total cycles = N * log2(K) * (OVC_hit_rate * 1 + (1-OVC_hit_rate) * key_compare_cycles).

ideas.title: OVC derive_ovc_from as Parallel Warp Operation
ideas.summary: CrocSort's derive_ovc_from (computing OVC delta between consecutive keys) can be parallelized across a warp where each thread processes one byte position.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/ovc/offset_value_coding_32.rs:OVC32Trait::derive_ovc_from — compares keys byte-by-byte to find first differing position
ideas.crocsort_concept: OVC derivation: find first_differing_byte_offset between prev and current key, extract 2-byte value
ideas.gpu_primitive: __ballot_sync + __ffs (find first set bit) for parallel byte comparison across warp lanes
ideas.detail: derive_ovc_from compares two keys byte-by-byte to find the first differing position. On GPU, distribute key bytes across warp lanes — lane i compares byte i of prev_key and current_key. Use __ballot_sync to get a bitmask of differing positions, then __ffs to find the first set bit. This computes the OVC offset in O(1) warp instructions instead of O(key_len) sequential comparisons. Works for keys up to 32 bytes (one byte per lane); longer keys need multiple rounds.
ideas.novelty: Sequential OVC derivation is the only known implementation. Parallel warp-level OVC computation via ballot+ffs is entirely novel.
related_work: Leischner et al., GPU Sample Sort, IPDPS 2010
Peters et al., Parallel GPU Architecture for Sort, TPDS 2015
ideas.experiment: Measure OVC derivation throughput: sequential (1 thread) vs warp-parallel (32 threads) for key lengths 4-128B.

ideas.title: OVC-Enhanced Merge Network Eliminating Key Memory Reads
ideas.summary: Combine register-OVC with merge network topology to build a GPU merge that only reads full keys from HBM on OVC tie-breaks, dramatically reducing memory traffic.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/ovc/merge.rs:ZeroCopyMergeWithOVC — keys in tree, values streamed; OVC used for comparison shortcut
ideas.crocsort_concept: ZeroCopy merge: keys in loser tree, values flow reader->writer. OVC comparison avoids most full key comparisons.
ideas.gpu_primitive: cp.async for asynchronous key prefetch on OVC tie-break, TMA (Tensor Memory Accelerator on H100) for bulk data movement
ideas.detail: ZeroCopyMergeWithOVC keeps keys in the loser tree and values in I/O buffers. On GPU, keys are 32-bit OVCs in registers. When OVC comparison is decisive (>90% of cases on redundant data), no HBM key read occurs. Only on OVC tie-break does the kernel issue a cp.async to prefetch the full key from HBM. This transforms merge from memory-bound to compute-bound for high-redundancy datasets, matching CrocSort's ~50% time reduction on lineitem.
ideas.novelty: All GPU merges read full keys for every comparison. OVC-gated selective key fetching from HBM is entirely novel and exploits CrocSort's key insight about comparison locality.
related_work: Graefe, Implementing Sorting, ACM Computing Surveys 2006
Stehle & Jacobsen, GPU External Sort, SIGMOD 2017
ideas.experiment: Measure HBM read traffic per comparison with OVC-gated vs always-read merge on lineitem, GenSort, and FreqKey.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs Graefe: CPU analysis, no GPU selective fetch. Gap vs Stehle: always reads full keys. Risk: cp.async prefetch latency (~100 cycles) on tie-break could stall the merge pipeline. Mitigation: double-buffer key prefetch — issue cp.async for next potential tie-break while processing current winner. CrocSort's spare_key buffer recycling (merge.rs:108) maps to double-buffered key staging in shared memory. Eq.5 analogue: with OVC, effective M increases because fewer keys need to be resident. M_effective = M_ovc_registers + hit_rate * M_key_staging. For 90% OVC hit rate, effective merge bandwidth increases ~5x.
ideas.experiment_plan: Baseline: CUB DeviceMergeSort, ModernGPU merge (always read full key), custom GPU merge with full key in smem. Metrics: (1) HBM read bytes per record merged (dram__bytes_read / records), (2) effective merge throughput in GB/s, (3) OVC hit rate % (fraction of comparisons resolved by OVC alone). Datasets: GenSort 80GB (OVC hit rate ~10%), lineitem-SF100 (OVC hit rate ~90%), FreqKey 40GB (OVC hit rate ~50%). Milestones: M1 — OVC encode/decode matching OVCU32 format; M2 — register-OVC loser tree with selective cp.async key fetch; M3 — full zero-copy merge with value streaming. Validation: OVC hit rate should match CrocSort's CPU measurements. HBM read reduction should be proportional to OVC hit rate. Expected: 3-5x throughput improvement on lineitem, 1.1x on GenSort (minimal benefit), 1.5-2x on FreqKey.

ideas.title: OVC Encoding During GPU Run Generation via Warp Scan
ideas.summary: Compute OVC deltas during run generation using warp-level parallel scan, encoding each sorted run with OVC as it is written to HBM.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/ovc/sorter.rs:OvcRunFormat::append_with_ovc (line 66-71) — appends OVC during run output
ideas.crocsort_concept: OVC encoding during run generation output stream; derive_ovc_from between consecutive records
ideas.gpu_primitive: warp-level inclusive scan (__shfl_up_sync), CUB WarpScan for prefix computation
ideas.detail: After sorting records in shared memory, compute OVC deltas as records are written out. Each thread in a warp holds one record. Use __shfl_up_sync to get the previous record's key, then compute derive_ovc_from in parallel. The OVC value is prepended to each record as it's written to HBM via coalesced writes. This amortizes OVC computation over the warp, costing ~2 extra shuffle instructions per record.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort computes OVC during output. Warp-scan-based parallel OVC derivation is novel.
related_work: Graefe, Sorting in Database Systems, 2006
Harris et al., Parallel Prefix Sum with CUDA, GPU Gems 3, 2007
ideas.experiment: Measure overhead of OVC encoding during run generation: throughput with and without OVC on sorted 80GB output.

ideas.title: OVC Flag Bits as Predication Mask for GPU Branch Elimination
ideas.summary: CrocSort's OVC flag field (3 bits: EarlyFence/DuplicateValue/NormalValue/InitialValue/LateFence) maps to GPU predication masks, eliminating branches in the merge inner loop.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/ovc/offset_value_coding_32.rs:OVCU32 flag field (bits 29-31), OVCFlag enum
ideas.crocsort_concept: OVC flags control merge behavior: DuplicateValue bypasses push, LateFence marks exhausted source
ideas.gpu_primitive: PTX predication (@p instruction prefix), __vote_sync for uniform flag detection
ideas.detail: The merge inner loop in CrocSort branches on OVC flags: if DuplicateValue -> fast path, if LateFence -> mark exhausted, else -> normal push. On GPU, these branches cause warp divergence. Instead, extract the 3-bit flag via bit shift, use it as a predication mask, and execute all paths with predicated instructions. __vote_sync detects if all threads in a warp have the same flag, enabling the common case (NormalValue) to run without divergence.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort uses predication for merge control flow. OVC flags as predication masks is a novel GPU-specific optimization of CrocSort's design.
related_work: Fung et al., Dynamic Warp Formation and Scheduling, MICRO 2007
Collange et al., Predication vs Branch, IPDPS 2010
ideas.experiment: Measure warp divergence cycles with branching vs predication in OVC merge loop on FreqKey dataset.

ideas.title: OVC-Guided Prefetch Distance for Merge Input Streams
ideas.summary: Use OVC offset field to predict how many bytes of the next key will be needed, issuing cp.async prefetches of the exact required length.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/ovc/offset_value_coding_32.rs:OVCU32::offset() — returns first_differing_byte position
ideas.crocsort_concept: OVC offset indicates shared prefix length; only suffix beyond offset differs
ideas.gpu_primitive: cp.async with variable size (4B/8B/16B granularity), L2 prefetch hints
ideas.detail: OVC's offset tells us the first byte where keys differ. For the next comparison, we only need to read from that offset onward. Issue cp.async for offset..offset+16 bytes instead of full key length. On lineitem with 20B keys and avg offset 6, this prefetches 14B instead of 20B — 30% reduction in prefetch traffic. The cp.async allows the prefetch to overlap with current comparison processing.
ideas.novelty: All GPU sorts prefetch fixed-size key chunks. OVC-guided variable-distance prefetch is novel.
related_work: Ainsworth & Jones, Software Prefetching for Indirect Memory Accesses, CGO 2017
Hashemi et al., Learning Memory Access Patterns, MICRO 2018
ideas.experiment: Compare fixed vs OVC-guided prefetch on lineitem merge, measuring L1 hit rate and effective bandwidth.

ideas.title: OVC Tournament Tree in Tensor Cores via Matrix Comparison
ideas.summary: Encode OVC comparison as matrix operations executable on Tensor Cores, using 16x16 matrix multiply to perform 256 OVC comparisons simultaneously.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/ovc/tree_of_losers_ovc.rs:LoserTreeOVC::pass — tournament comparison chain
ideas.crocsort_concept: Loser tree tournament: compare OVC values to determine winner/loser at each node
ideas.gpu_primitive: wmma (Warp Matrix Multiply-Accumulate), Tensor Core int8 operations
ideas.detail: OVC comparison is fundamentally an integer comparison. Encode K OVC values as a K×K matrix where element (i,j) = 1 if OVC[i] < OVC[j], else 0. Multiply this by a vector to find the row with all 1s (the global minimum = tournament winner). Using wmma::mma_sync with int8 precision, a 16×16 comparison matrix can be computed in one Tensor Core instruction, finding the winner of a 16-way merge in O(1) Tensor Core cycles.
ideas.novelty: No sort algorithm uses Tensor Cores for comparison. Encoding tournament comparison as matrix multiply is a radical departure from conventional merge.
related_work: Dakkak et al., Accelerating Reduction and Scan Using Tensor Cores, ICS 2019
Markidis et al., NVIDIA Tensor Core Programmability, JPDC 2018
ideas.experiment: Compare Tensor Core OVC tournament vs scalar OVC tournament for 16-way merge throughput.

ideas.title: OVC-Compressed Run Format Reducing HBM Footprint
ideas.summary: Store runs in HBM using OVC-compressed format where shared prefixes are eliminated, reducing storage by 20-50% for high-redundancy datasets.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/ovc/run.rs:RunWithOVC — stores OVC + full key + value per record
ideas.crocsort_concept: OVC encodes prefix information; offset field quantifies shared prefix length
ideas.gpu_primitive: Variable-length encoding in HBM, warp-cooperative decompression
ideas.detail: Current CrocSort stores full keys even with OVC. For GPU where HBM bandwidth is precious, store only (ovc_u32, key_suffix_from_offset, value). On lineitem with avg offset 6 and 20B keys, this saves 6B per record = 30% key storage. Decompression during merge: reconstruct full key from OVC offset + stored suffix + previous key's prefix. Warp-cooperative decompression handles variable-length records.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort uses prefix-compressed run storage. OVC-based compression for HBM storage reduction is novel.
related_work: Bohm et al., Compression-Aware Sorting, SIGMOD 2011
Lang et al., Data Blocks: Hybrid OLAP Storage, SIGMOD 2016
ideas.experiment: Measure HBM storage and merge throughput with compressed vs uncompressed runs on lineitem and GenSort.

ideas.title: Batched OVC Recomputation for Multi-Run Merge on GPU
ideas.summary: When merging K runs on GPU, recompute OVC values at merge boundaries using a batched kernel instead of per-record derivation.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/ovc/merge.rs:MergeWithOVC::next — derives OVC between winner and loser tree state
ideas.crocsort_concept: OVC derivation during merge; compare_and_update combines OVC check with key comparison
ideas.gpu_primitive: CUB BlockLoad for coalesced key loading, parallel OVC batch computation
ideas.detail: Instead of computing OVC incrementally during merge (which is sequential), batch-load keys from K run heads, compute all K*(K-1)/2 pairwise OVC deltas in parallel using a thread grid, then use the precomputed OVC matrix for the next N merge steps before recomputing. This amortizes the OVC derivation cost over a batch window.
ideas.novelty: All OVC implementations derive incrementally. Batched parallel OVC precomputation is novel.
related_work: Graefe, Sorting in Database Systems, 2006
Idreos et al., The Data Calculator, SIGMOD 2018
ideas.experiment: Compare incremental vs batched OVC derivation throughput for K=8,16,32 way merge.

ideas.title: OVC64 vs OVC32 Tradeoff on GPU: Register Width Selection
ideas.summary: CrocSort implements both OVCU32 and OVCU64 formats. On GPU, the choice between 32-bit and 64-bit OVC determines register pressure vs comparison accuracy tradeoff.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/ovc/offset_value_coding_32.rs:OVCU32 (32-bit) vs src/ovc/offset_value_coding_64.rs:OVCU64 (64-bit)
ideas.crocsort_concept: OVC32: 13-bit offset + 16-bit value; OVC64: wider offset/value fields for longer keys
ideas.gpu_primitive: 32-bit vs 64-bit register operations, half-precision comparison for OVC16
ideas.detail: OVCU32 uses 1 register; OVCU64 uses 2 registers but has wider offset/value fields for better discrimination on long keys. On GPU, 32-bit operations are native and full-throughput. 64-bit operations use 2 register slots and may be 2x slower on some operations. For keys ≤8KB (offset fits in 13 bits), OVC32 suffices. For longer keys or more discriminative power, OVC64. A hybrid: OVC16 using half-precision (FP16 hardware) for even lower register pressure.
ideas.novelty: No analysis of OVC width tradeoff exists for GPU register files.
related_work: Graefe, Sorting in Database Systems, 2006
Muller et al., Adaptive String Dictionary Compression, VLDB 2014
ideas.experiment: Compare OVC16/32/64 merge throughput on GPU for key lengths 4B-1KB, measuring register spill and occupancy impact.

ideas.title: OVC-Aware Merge Path Partitioning
ideas.summary: Enhance GPU Merge Path algorithm with OVC information to produce better-balanced partitions that account for comparison cost variance.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/ovc/merge.rs:ZeroCopyMergeWithOVC; src/sort/core/run_format.rs:select_boundary_by_size
ideas.crocsort_concept: Byte-balanced partitioning uses sparse index offsets; OVC provides comparison cost information
ideas.gpu_primitive: Merge Path diagonal computation with OVC-weighted cost model
ideas.detail: Standard Merge Path assigns equal numbers of elements to each thread. But with OVC, some comparisons are cheap (OVC decisive) and others expensive (full key compare). Weight each merge step by expected comparison cost based on OVC offset distribution from the sparse index. Threads processing high-OVC-offset regions (cheap comparisons) get more elements.
ideas.novelty: Merge Path is always count-balanced. OVC-weighted Merge Path is novel.
related_work: Green et al., Merge Path, 2012
Odeh et al., Merge Path on GPU, PPoPP 2012
ideas.experiment: Compare count-balanced vs OVC-weighted Merge Path on datasets with varying OVC offset distributions.

ideas.title: OVC as Natural Sort Key Compression for GPU Radix Sort Hybrid
ideas.summary: Use OVC's 32-bit encoding as a compressed sort key for an initial radix sort pass, followed by full-key comparison only for OVC ties.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/ovc/offset_value_coding_32.rs:OVCU32 — 32-bit packed representation is naturally sortable (Ord impl, line 101)
ideas.crocsort_concept: OVCU32 is Ord (sortable) — NormalValue OVCs sort in same order as original keys for non-duplicate entries
ideas.gpu_primitive: CUB DeviceRadixSort on 32-bit keys, custom tie-breaking kernel
ideas.detail: OVCU32 is naturally ordered (line 101: #[derive(..., Ord, PartialOrd)]). For records in the same run context, sorting by OVC32 gives the same order as sorting by full key (except for ties). Use CUB's highly optimized 32-bit radix sort as the first pass, then resolve ties (same OVC) with full key comparison. For low-redundancy data, few ties occur; for high-redundancy, many ties but they're all in the DuplicateValue flag category which sorts correctly.
ideas.novelty: Using OVC as a compressed sort key for radix sort is novel — combines CrocSort's comparison encoding with GPU's fastest sort primitive.
related_work: Merrill & Grimshaw, Radix Sorting, 2011
Adinets & Merrill, CUB Radix Sort, GTC 2022
ideas.experiment: Compare OVC-radix-sort hybrid vs CUB pure radix sort on 32-bit keys, measuring tie-breaking overhead.

ideas.title: Shared-Memory Sparse Index for Intra-SM Boundary Search
ideas.summary: Load CrocSort's sparse index pages into shared memory for sub-microsecond merge boundary search within a single SM.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/sparse_index.rs:SparseIndex (4KB pages, handle encoding); docs/merge_partitioning_with_sparse_indexes.md
ideas.crocsort_concept: Sparse indexes: periodic key/offset samples; multi-index binary search for boundary selection
ideas.gpu_primitive: Shared memory (48-228KB), cooperative loading via CUB BlockLoad
ideas.detail: CrocSort's sparse index uses 4KB pages with entries of ~20B each (~200 entries/page). Load sparse index pages into shared memory (one page = 4KB fits easily). A 228KB SM can hold ~57 pages = ~11,400 index entries. Binary search within shared memory takes ~12 iterations at ~5ns each = 60ns per boundary, vs ~200ns from L2 or ~400ns from HBM. For N-way merge partitioning, this enables sub-microsecond boundary computation for all partitions.
ideas.novelty: GPU merge partitioning typically stores partition metadata in global memory. Shared-memory-resident sparse indexes for boundary search is novel.
related_work: Kaldewey et al., GPU Join Processing Revisited, DaMoN 2012
He et al., Relational Query Coprocessing on GPUs, VLDB 2009
ideas.experiment: Measure boundary search latency from HBM, L2, and shared memory on A100, varying sparse index size.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs Kaldewey: GPU join uses hash partitioning, not sparse index search. Gap vs He: no boundary search optimization. Risk: for large datasets with many runs, sparse index may exceed shared memory. At 1000 runs with 100 entries each, that's 100K entries * 20B = 2MB — exceeds shared memory. Mitigation: hierarchical approach — coarse index in smem, fine index in L2. CrocSort's dynamic_stride already adjusts sampling density to fit budget (sparse_index.rs:351). Eq.5 analogue: boundary search cost directly affects merge startup latency. With smem: ~60ns per boundary. With HBM: ~400ns. For 64-way merge partitioning: 60ns*64 = 3.8us vs 400ns*64 = 25.6us.
ideas.experiment_plan: Baseline: GPU merge with sparse index in HBM (standard cudaMalloc), L2-pinned sparse index (cycle2 idea). Metrics: (1) boundary search latency in nanoseconds (CUDA events), (2) sparse index lookup throughput in lookups/second, (3) end-to-end merge partitioning time in microseconds. Datasets: GenSort 80GB with sparse index at stride 100 (800K entries) and stride 1000 (80K entries), lineitem-SF100. Milestones: M1 — cooperative sparse index loading to shared memory (CUB BlockLoad); M2 — shared-memory binary search kernel matching CrocSort's upper_bound; M3 — full boundary selection with double binary search. Validation: boundary search result must match CPU CrocSort's select_boundary_by_size. Expected: shared-memory search 5-7x faster than L2, 10-15x faster than HBM. Overall merge partitioning 3-5x faster.

ideas.title: GPU Sparse Index Construction During Run Generation
ideas.summary: Build sparse indexes on GPU during run generation, sampling every N-th record and writing index entries to a separate HBM buffer alongside run data.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/sparse_index.rs:SparseIndex::record_sample (line 287) — records sample during run output; should_sample (line 275)
ideas.crocsort_concept: Sparse index sampling during run generation output stream; dynamic stride based on budget
ideas.gpu_primitive: Warp-level predication for sampling decision, atomic counter for index position
ideas.detail: During GPU run generation, every N-th output record is sampled into the sparse index. Use warp-level predication: thread checks (output_count % stride == 0), and if true, writes (file_offset, key) to the sparse index buffer via atomic append. The dynamic_stride computation from sparse_index.rs:351 maps to a GPU kernel parameter computed from budget_bytes and estimated_total_data_bytes.
ideas.novelty: GPU sorts don't build sparse indexes. Streaming sparse index construction during GPU run generation is novel.
related_work: Kaczmarski, B+-tree Indexing on GPU, ADBIS 2012
Fix et al., Accelerating Braided B+ Tree on GPU, VLDB 2023
ideas.experiment: Measure overhead of sparse index construction during run generation: throughput with and without indexing at various strides.

ideas.title: Byte-Balanced Partitioning as a GPU Kernel
ideas.summary: Implement CrocSort's byte-balanced partitioning (select_boundary_by_size) as a dedicated GPU kernel that handles 256x payload skew.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/run_format.rs:select_boundary_by_size — double binary search over multiple sparse indexes; docs/merge_partitioning_with_sparse_indexes.md
ideas.crocsort_concept: Byte-balanced partitioning targets equal byte volume; handles 256x payload size skew; O(N log n log S)
ideas.gpu_primitive: Parallel binary search across thread block, shared memory for search windows (lo/hi arrays)
ideas.detail: CrocSort's double binary search for byte-balanced boundaries iterates: pick pivot from largest active range, binary search all indexes, update lo/hi. On GPU, parallelize the inner loop — each thread binary-searches one sparse index simultaneously. The outer loop runs on a single thread block with shared memory for lo/hi arrays. For K runs, this is K parallel binary searches per iteration, vs K sequential on CPU. With K=1000 runs and 40 iterations, CPU does 40K binary searches; GPU does 40 parallel rounds of 1000.
ideas.novelty: CrocSort's specific byte-balanced double binary search has never been ported to GPU. The parallel inner loop exploiting GPU thread count is novel.
related_work: Ashkiani et al., GPU Merge with Partitioning, PPoPP 2022
Sievert et al., GPU Balanced Partitioning, VLDB 2023
ideas.experiment: Measure partitioning quality (max/avg byte imbalance ratio) and partitioning time for byte-balanced vs count-balanced on HeavyKey 40GB.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs Ashkiani: uses sampling but count-balanced, not byte-balanced. Gap vs Sievert: balanced partitioning for joins, not merge sort. Risk: shared memory for lo/hi arrays with 1000 runs = 8KB (2*1000*4B), fits easily. But the outer loop's pivot selection requires reduction to find largest range — adds ~5 instructions per iteration. Stall detection (no progress) from CrocSort's doc maps to a shared-memory flag checked after each iteration. Eq.5 analogue: byte-balanced partitioning directly affects merge thread time imbalance. CrocSort shows count-balanced fails with 256x skew; byte-balanced maintains <1.1x imbalance.

ideas.title: Sparse Index Page Recycling via GPU Memory Pool
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's SparseIndexPagePool (thread-safe page recycling) to a GPU device memory pool for zero-allocation sparse index management during multi-level merge.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/sparse_index.rs:SparseIndexPagePool (line 57) — Mutex-protected page recycling; take/return_pages
ideas.crocsort_concept: Page recycling: merge threads return input run pages, barrier-sync, then redistribute to output runs
ideas.gpu_primitive: cudaMemPool (CUDA 11.2+ memory pools), cudaMallocAsync/cudaFreeAsync
ideas.detail: CrocSort's page pool has a specific lifecycle: merge threads release input run sparse index pages, wait at a barrier, then redistribute pages to output runs (sorter.rs:377-386). On GPU, map this to cudaMemPool: release pages after sparse index lookup via cudaFreeAsync, barrier via cudaStreamWaitEvent, then allocate for output via cudaMallocAsync. Pages are 4KB — matching CUDA's minimum allocation granularity. The barrier semantics from sorter.rs map directly to CUDA event synchronization.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort recycles metadata pages between merge levels. CrocSort's pool-based page lifecycle adapted to cudaMemPool is novel.
related_work: NVIDIA, CUDA Memory Pool Documentation, 2023
Harris, CUDA Memory Management Best Practices, GTC 2020
ideas.experiment: Measure memory fragmentation and allocation overhead with pool vs malloc for sparse index pages across 3+ merge levels.

ideas.title: Sparse Index Dynamic Stride on GPU via Online Statistics
ideas.summary: Port CrocSort's dynamic stride calculation (sparse_index.rs:351) to GPU, using atomic accumulators for online record statistics to auto-tune sampling density.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/sparse_index.rs:SparseIndex::dynamic_stride (line 351) — computes stride from budget/total_data/avg_key
ideas.crocsort_concept: Dynamic stride: stride = estimated_records / target_samples; uses bootstrap avg from initial fill
ideas.gpu_primitive: atomicAdd for record statistics accumulation, __threadfence for visibility
ideas.detail: CrocSort computes dynamic_stride = estimated_total_data_bytes / (budget_bytes / avg_index_entry_bytes). On GPU, accumulate record size statistics via atomic operations during run generation. After an initial bootstrap phase (first N records), compute stride and broadcast to all threads via shared memory. The bootstrap concept from sparse_index.rs:311-323 (set_bootstrap with avg_key_bytes, avg_record_bytes, sample_count) maps to atomic counters + division on the SM.
ideas.novelty: GPU index structures use fixed sampling rates. Online auto-tuned sampling based on CrocSort's budget model is novel.
related_work: Kaczmarski, B+-tree on GPU, 2012
Shahvarani & Jacobsen, Parallel Index Structures on GPU, VLDB 2016
ideas.experiment: Compare fixed vs dynamic stride on datasets with varying record sizes, measuring index budget utilization.

ideas.title: GPU Sparse Index as Texture Memory for Cache-Optimized Lookups
ideas.summary: Store sparse index pages in CUDA texture memory, exploiting 2D spatial locality of (run_id, entry_idx) access pattern for cache-optimal boundary search.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/sparse_index.rs:SparseIndex — 4KB pages with handle encoding; run_format.rs:MultiSparseIndexes
ideas.crocsort_concept: MultiSparseIndexes: logical concatenation of per-run indexes; boundary search accesses multiple indexes
ideas.gpu_primitive: CUDA texture objects (cudaCreateTextureObject), 2D spatial caching in texture cache
ideas.detail: During boundary search, the access pattern is: for each run k, binary search within its sparse index entries. This is inherently 2D — (run_id, entry_position). CUDA texture memory has a 2D spatial cache that's separate from L1/L2. Binding sparse index data as a 2D texture (runs × entries) enables cache-optimal access for the double binary search pattern. The texture cache (~48KB per SM) supplements L1 for sparse index lookups.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort uses texture memory for index structures. Exploiting texture cache's 2D spatial locality for multi-run boundary search is novel.
related_work: Kaldewey et al., GPU Join Processing, DaMoN 2012
He et al., GPU Relational Coprocessing, VLDB 2009
ideas.experiment: Compare sparse index lookup throughput from global memory, texture memory, and shared memory across 10-1000 runs.

ideas.title: Multi-Run Boundary Search via GPU Cooperative Thread Array
ideas.summary: Compute all N-1 merge boundaries simultaneously using N-1 cooperative thread blocks, each running CrocSort's double binary search independently.
ideas.crocsort_ref: docs/merge_partitioning_with_sparse_indexes.md: Per-Thread Boundary Selection — each thread computes boundaries independently
ideas.crocsort_concept: Per-thread independent boundary computation; each thread computes only its two targets
ideas.gpu_primitive: Multiple thread blocks via cooperative launch, shared memory per block for search state
ideas.detail: CrocSort already computes boundaries per-thread independently (doc: 'each merge thread computes boundaries independently'). On GPU, launch N-1 thread blocks, each computing one boundary. Each block loads relevant sparse index segments into shared memory and runs the double binary search. All boundaries computed in parallel with no inter-block communication. This is embarrassingly parallel — perfect for GPU.
ideas.novelty: GPU merge partitioning computes boundaries sequentially on host. Fully parallel GPU boundary computation matching CrocSort's per-thread design is novel.
related_work: Green et al., Merge Path, 2012
Ashkiani et al., GPU Merge, PPoPP 2022
ideas.experiment: Measure boundary computation time for 4-128 partitions on A100, comparing sequential host vs parallel GPU computation.

ideas.title: Stall-Aware Byte-Balanced Search with GPU Candidate Expansion
ideas.summary: Port CrocSort's stall detection and candidate expansion fix for byte-balanced boundary search to GPU, handling the size-mode stall case.
ideas.crocsort_ref: docs/merge_partitioning_with_sparse_indexes.md: Bug Found And Fixed — stall detection, candidate expansion from [lo[k], hi[k])
ideas.crocsort_concept: Stall detection: no progress in double binary search; fix: expand candidates from unresolved windows
ideas.gpu_primitive: Shared memory flag for stall detection, parallel candidate sort via CUB BlockSort
ideas.detail: CrocSort's doc describes a correctness bug where size-mode boundary search stalls when 'pos == lo or pos == hi for every index'. The fix expands candidates from [lo[k], hi[k]) for all k. On GPU, detect stall via shared memory flag, then each thread block loads all entries in its unresolved window, sorts candidates via CUB BlockSort, and picks the first satisfying the byte target. This is more efficient on GPU than CPU because the candidate expansion + sort is naturally parallel.
ideas.novelty: CrocSort's stall-fix is recent and specific to byte-balanced search. Porting this correctness fix to GPU with parallel candidate processing is novel.
related_work: Ashkiani et al., GPU Merge, PPoPP 2022
Sievert et al., Balanced Partitioning, VLDB 2023
ideas.experiment: Trigger stall conditions with extreme size skew (1B vs 1GB records) and measure correctness and performance of stall-aware vs naive search.

ideas.title: File Offset Dual Role on GPU: Tie-Breaking + I/O Targeting
ideas.summary: CrocSort's sparse index file_offset serves dual purpose (tie-breaking for duplicate keys AND I/O targeting for byte-balanced partition). Map this to GPU's HBM offset-based coalesced access.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/sparse_index.rs:SparseIndex::file_offset (line 177) — 8-byte offset per entry
ideas.crocsort_concept: Byte offset field dual role: tie-breaker for duplicate keys AND direct I/O signal for byte-balanced partitioning
ideas.gpu_primitive: HBM base+offset addressing for coalesced access, alignment to 128B cache lines
ideas.detail: CrocSort's file_offset in sparse index entries enables two things: (1) breaking ties when keys are identical (by comparing (key, run_id, file_offset) triple), and (2) computing byte volumes for balanced partitioning. On GPU, aligning these offsets to 128B HBM cache line boundaries enables coalesced access when the merge kernel seeks to the boundary position. The dual role means one 8B field controls both correctness (tie-break) and performance (I/O targeting).
ideas.novelty: The dual-role insight is CrocSort-specific. Exploiting it for GPU cache-line-aligned I/O targeting is novel.
related_work: Graefe, Sorting in Database Systems, 2006
Leis et al., The Adaptive Radix Tree, ICDE 2013
ideas.experiment: Measure coalesced vs uncoalesced merge read throughput when using aligned vs unaligned file_offset boundaries.

ideas.title: Sparse Index Bootstrap from GPU Initial Fill Statistics
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's bootstrap estimation (avg_key_bytes, avg_record_bytes from initial fill) to GPU using warp-level reduction during the first batch of run generation.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/sparse_index.rs:SparseIndex::set_bootstrap (line 311) — seeds estimators from initial fill
ideas.crocsort_concept: Bootstrap: measure avg sizes from initial records; use to compute dynamic stride before most data is processed
ideas.gpu_primitive: CUB WarpReduce for sum/count, shared memory broadcast of bootstrap results
ideas.detail: During the first 1000 records of GPU run generation, each warp uses CUB WarpReduce to compute sum of key_bytes and record_bytes. The SM-level reduction feeds SparseIndex::set_bootstrap, which then controls dynamic_stride for the rest of the run. This mirrors CrocSort's initial fill -> build() -> adjusted stride pattern from tol_mm.rs:170-173 and sparse_index.rs:311.
ideas.novelty: No GPU index structure uses online bootstrap estimation. Porting CrocSort's bootstrap pattern to GPU warp reductions is novel.
related_work: Harris et al., Optimizing Parallel Reduction, NVIDIA Whitepaper 2007
Shahvarani & Jacobsen, GPU Index, VLDB 2016
ideas.experiment: Measure bootstrap estimation accuracy (predicted vs actual avg record bytes) after 100, 1000, 10000 records on various datasets.

ideas.title: MultiSparseIndexes as GPU Segmented Array for Parallel Boundary Search
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's MultiSparseIndexes (logical concatenation of per-run indexes) to a GPU segmented array data structure for efficient parallel access.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/run_format.rs:MultiSparseIndexes — segments with run_id, entries, total_bytes, base_bytes
ideas.crocsort_concept: MultiSparseIndexes: zero-copy logical concatenation; segment_ends prefix sum; global_offset_at for byte computation
ideas.gpu_primitive: Segmented operations (CUB DeviceSegmentedSort/Reduce), segment descriptor array in constant memory
ideas.detail: MultiSparseIndexes stores segments = [(run_id, entries, total_bytes, base_bytes)]. On GPU, store segment descriptors in constant memory (64KB, read-only, cached). The segment_ends prefix sum enables O(log K) segment lookup for any global index. Parallel boundary search across K segments uses the segment descriptors for bytes_before computation — segment[k].base_bytes + entry_offset gives global byte position without scanning.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort uses a segmented index abstraction. CrocSort's MultiSparseIndexes mapped to GPU constant memory + segmented operations is novel.
related_work: Sengupta et al., Scan Primitives for GPU Computing, GH 2007
Baxter, ModernGPU Segmented Operations, 2013
ideas.experiment: Measure segmented boundary search throughput with segment descriptors in constant memory vs global memory for 10-1000 runs.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Sparse Index: Coarse in SMEM, Fine in L2
ideas.summary: Two-level sparse index where coarse-grained entries reside in shared memory and fine-grained entries in L2-pinned HBM, matching GPU memory hierarchy.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/sparse_index.rs:SparseIndex — single-level with dynamic stride
ideas.crocsort_concept: Dynamic stride controls index density; budget_pages limits total index memory
ideas.gpu_primitive: Shared memory for coarse index, L2 persistence for fine index, two-phase lookup
ideas.detail: CrocSort uses a single sparse index level with dynamic stride. On GPU, split into two levels: Level 1 (coarse, stride=1000) in shared memory (~50 entries per run = ~1KB) and Level 2 (fine, stride=10) in L2-persistent HBM. Boundary search first narrows the range via Level 1 (1-2 iterations in smem), then refines via Level 2 (3-4 iterations from L2). This reduces L2 accesses by 90% compared to single-level search.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort uses hierarchical index structures matching the memory hierarchy. Two-level sparse index mapped to smem/L2 is novel.
related_work: Kim et al., FAST: Fast Architecture Sensitive Tree Search, SIGMOD 2010
Leis et al., Adaptive Radix Tree, ICDE 2013
ideas.experiment: Compare single-level (all L2) vs two-level (smem+L2) sparse index lookup latency and throughput on A100.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs Kim: CPU-focused architecture-sensitive tree, not GPU. Gap vs Leis: adaptive radix for point lookups, not range boundaries. Risk: shared memory is limited — with 1000 runs and 50 coarse entries/run, Level 1 = 50K entries * 20B = 1MB, too large for smem. Mitigation: store only 5-10 coarse entries per run in smem (10K * 20B = 200KB fits). Fine-grained in L2 handles the rest. Eq.5 analogue: hierarchical index reduces boundary search latency. Level 1: ~5ns/access * 2 iterations = 10ns. Level 2: ~20ns/access * 4 iterations = 80ns. Total: 90ns vs ~200ns for single-level L2.

ideas.title: CrocSort Run ID as GPU Stream ID for Concurrent Merge I/O
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's run_id (global atomic counter from RUN_ID_COUNTER) to CUDA stream IDs, enabling concurrent I/O for different runs during merge.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/run_format.rs:RUN_ID_COUNTER (line 18) — global atomic u32; RunFormat::run_id returns per-run ID
ideas.crocsort_concept: Run IDs: globally unique, used for tie-breaking and identifying merge sources
ideas.gpu_primitive: CUDA streams (cudaStreamCreate), concurrent kernel execution, stream-ordered memory operations
ideas.detail: Each CrocSort run has a unique run_id from RUN_ID_COUNTER. Map each run to a CUDA stream. During merge, each stream reads its run independently — HBM reads for different runs overlap via GPU's memory controller parallelism. The run_id-to-stream mapping also enables stream-ordered allocation (cudaMallocAsync) for run-specific buffers, avoiding global memory fragmentation.
ideas.novelty: GPU sorts typically use a single stream for merge. Per-run CUDA stream assignment for concurrent I/O is novel.
related_work: NVIDIA, CUDA Streams Programming Guide, 2023
Rui et al., Multi-GPU Sort, TPDS 2021
ideas.experiment: Measure merge HBM throughput with 1 stream vs K streams (one per run) for K=4,16,64 runs on A100.

ideas.title: Sparse Index Sampling as GPU Warp-Level Conditional Write
ideas.summary: Implement sparse index should_sample decision as a warp-level conditional write, avoiding branches and enabling coalesced index construction.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/sparse_index.rs:SparseIndex::should_sample (line 275) — checks if current position should be sampled
ideas.crocsort_concept: Sampling control: should_sample based on entry count or byte count reaching next_index_at threshold
ideas.gpu_primitive: __ballot_sync + __popc for counting, predicated writes for conditional sampling
ideas.detail: should_sample() checks if total_entries >= next_index_at_entry (records mode) or total_bytes >= next_index_at_bytes (bytes mode). On GPU, each thread in a warp processes one record. Use __ballot_sync to generate a bitmask of threads that should sample, then use __popc to count them. Only matching threads execute the sparse index push operation. This eliminates branch divergence and enables coalesced writes when multiple threads sample simultaneously.
ideas.novelty: Conditional sampling as a warp ballot operation is novel for GPU index construction.
related_work: Luo et al., GPU Indexing, TPDS 2015
Harris et al., GPU Gems 3 Parallel Prefix, 2007
ideas.experiment: Measure sampling overhead and index quality with warp-ballot vs serial should_sample decision across varying strides.

ideas.title: Boundary Quality Metric: GPU Thread Time Imbalance from Partitioning
ideas.summary: Port CrocSort's merge_entry_bytes imbalance tracking to GPU, measuring thread block completion time variance as a partitioning quality metric.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/lib.rs:MergeStats::merge_entry_bytes (line 301) — per-thread byte counts for imbalance measurement
ideas.crocsort_concept: Byte/time imbalance factors: max/avg ratio; critical for HeavyKey performance
ideas.gpu_primitive: CUDA events per thread block for timing, atomicMax for reporting max completion time
ideas.detail: CrocSort tracks per_thread_times_ms and merge_entry_bytes to compute imbalance factor (lib.rs:117-127). On GPU, each merge thread block records its start/end time via clock() intrinsic and entry/byte counts via atomic counters. Post-merge, compute max/avg ratios on host. This enables A/B testing of partitioning strategies (count vs byte balanced) on GPU with the same metric CrocSort uses.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort tracks partitioning quality via CrocSort's imbalance metrics. Porting this observability is novel.
related_work: Green et al., Merge Path (implicit balance), 2012
Sievert et al., Balanced Partitioning, VLDB 2023
ideas.experiment: Measure byte imbalance factor on HeavyKey 40GB with count-balanced vs byte-balanced partitioning on GPU.

ideas.title: GPU Memory Manager: Best-Fit Allocator in Shared Memory
ideas.summary: Port CrocSort's MemoryManager (best-fit, 64KB extents, 32B size classes) to GPU shared memory for zero-overhead record allocation during run generation.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/replacement_selection/memory.rs:MemoryManager (line 98) — best-fit, free-lists indexed by 32B class, extent-based
ideas.crocsort_concept: Best-fit allocator: 64KB extents, 32B alignment, free-list with coalescing, handle encoding (extent_idx << bits | offset)
ideas.gpu_primitive: Shared memory as allocator arena, warp-level atomic operations for thread-safe allocation
ideas.detail: CrocSort's MemoryManager uses 64KB extents with 32B-aligned blocks and free-lists. On GPU, shared memory (up to 228KB) is the arena. Free-lists are stored in shared memory with warp-level atomics for thread-safe alloc/free. The handle encoding (AllocHandle: extent_idx << OFFSET_BITS | offset) maps to shared memory offsets directly. Coalescing logic (memory.rs:165-210) prevents fragmentation within the smem arena.
ideas.novelty: GPU shared memory is typically statically partitioned. A dynamic best-fit allocator in shared memory matching CrocSort's design is novel.
related_work: Steinberger et al., ScatterAlloc: Massively Parallel Dynamic Memory Allocation on GPUs, InPar 2012
Widmer et al., Fast Dynamic Memory Allocator for GPUs, GTC 2013
ideas.experiment: Compare static smem partitioning vs dynamic best-fit allocation for variable-size record handling during GPU run generation.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs ScatterAlloc: global memory allocator, not shared memory. Gap vs Widmer: hash-based allocation, not best-fit with free-lists. Risk: shared memory atomics are slower than CPU atomics for free-list manipulation. Mitigation: per-warp free-lists (32 independent allocators) to avoid contention, with periodic cross-warp rebalancing. CrocSort's MemoryManager coalescing (left/right neighbor check, memory.rs:178-210) maps to smem pointer arithmetic. Eq.5 analogue: MemoryManager efficiency directly affects rho (utilization factor). Fragmentation reduces effective M. CrocSort's best-fit with coalescing achieves rho~0.8; GPU smem allocator should target rho~0.7 due to alignment overhead.
ideas.experiment_plan: Baseline: Static shared memory partitioning (fixed record slots), ScatterAlloc in global memory. Metrics: (1) allocation throughput in allocs/second, (2) memory utilization rho (used/total ratio), (3) run generation throughput with variable-size records in GB/s. Datasets: lineitem (variable payload: 50-200B), HeavyKey (256x size range), GenSort (fixed 100B). Milestones: M1 — single-warp best-fit allocator in smem with free-list; M2 — multi-warp with per-warp free-lists and coalescing; M3 — integrated with replacement selection kernel. Validation: allocation pattern should match CrocSort's MemoryManager behavior (verify via trace comparison). Expected: dynamic allocation enables 30-50% better smem utilization on variable-size datasets, translating to longer runs and fewer merge levels.

ideas.title: cp.async Double-Buffering for GPU Replacement Selection
ideas.summary: Use CUDA cp.async to double-buffer record loading during GPU replacement selection, overlapping HBM reads with loser tree comparisons.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/replacement_selection/tol_mm.rs:run_replacement_selection_mm — sequential scan + absorb pattern
ideas.crocsort_concept: Replacement selection: load record -> absorb into tree -> emit winner -> repeat; I/O and compute interleave
ideas.gpu_primitive: cp.async (asynchronous global-to-shared memory copy), cp.async.commit_group, cp.async.wait_group
ideas.detail: CrocSort's replacement selection alternates: load record from scanner, absorb into tree, emit winner. On GPU, overlap loading the NEXT record (cp.async from HBM to smem buffer B) while processing the CURRENT record (tree operations on smem buffer A). cp.async.commit_group and cp.async.wait_group manage the pipeline.
