This hides HBM latency (~400 cycles) behind tree comparison work (~50-100 cycles for OVC), keeping the SM utilized.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort uses cp.async for replacement selection pipelining. Double-buffered record loading with asynchronous copy is novel.
related_work: NVIDIA, cp.async Programming Guide, 2021
Dao et al., FlashAttention: IO-Aware Attention, NeurIPS 2022
ideas.experiment: Measure replacement selection throughput with and without cp.async double-buffering, varying record size and HBM latency.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs FlashAttention: uses cp.async for matrix tiles, not sort records. Gap vs NVIDIA guide: general async copy, not applied to replacement selection. Risk: cp.async requires shared memory staging buffers, reducing available smem for the loser tree. With double-buffering, 2 * max_record_size bytes are reserved. For 1KB records: 2KB overhead. For 10KB records: 20KB — significant on smaller smem configs. Mitigation: use triple-buffering only when records are small. Eq.5 analogue: cp.async transforms replacement selection from latency-bound to compute-bound. Effective throughput = min(compute_throughput, HBM_bandwidth/record_size). With overlap, achieves max(compute, HBM) instead of sum(compute_latency, HBM_latency).

ideas.title: Tree-of-Losers in Shared Memory with Bank-Conflict-Free Layout
ideas.summary: Layout CrocSort's loser tree in shared memory with padding to eliminate bank conflicts, enabling full-throughput tree operations.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/ovc/tree_of_losers.rs:LoserTree — nodes array with parent_index = index/2 navigation
ideas.crocsort_concept: Loser tree: capacity nodes, parent = index/2; Phase 1 climb + Phase 2 bubble-up
ideas.gpu_primitive: Shared memory bank conflict avoidance (32 banks, 4B stride), padding/swizzling
ideas.detail: CrocSort's loser tree stores nodes contiguously: nodes[0..capacity]. On GPU shared memory with 32 banks (4B each), consecutive tree levels access the same bank (index/2 pattern creates bank conflicts). Fix: pad each tree level to start at a different bank. For capacity=32 (5 levels), add 4B padding between levels. This increases tree memory by ~15% but eliminates 32-way bank conflicts that would serialize access. The swap_node operation (tree_of_losers.rs:140) becomes conflict-free.
ideas.novelty: Bank-conflict-free loser tree layout for GPU shared memory is novel. Standard GPU merge trees don't address bank conflicts.
related_work: Harris et al., Parallel Prefix Sum, GPU Gems 3, 2007
Yan et al., Bank Conflict-Free Shared Memory, MICRO 2009
ideas.experiment: Measure tree operation throughput with and without bank-conflict-free layout via NSight Compute bank conflict counter.

ideas.title: GPU Allocator Handle Encoding Matching CrocSort's AllocHandle
ideas.summary: Port CrocSort's AllocHandle encoding (extent_idx << OFFSET_BITS | byte_offset) to GPU, using 32-bit handles for O(1) smem access.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/replacement_selection/memory.rs:AllocHandle (line 23) — pack extent_index and offset_bytes into u32
ideas.crocsort_concept: AllocHandle: 32-bit packed handle; NONE/EARLY/LATE sentinels; extent_index + offset_bytes encoding
ideas.gpu_primitive: 32-bit shared memory addressing, bit manipulation intrinsics (__clz, __ffs)
ideas.detail: CrocSort's AllocHandle packs (extent_idx << OFFSET_BITS) | (offset/ALIGN) into a u32, with reserved sentinel values NONE=MAX, EARLY=MAX-1, LATE=MAX-2. On GPU, this encoding enables O(1) translation to shared memory address: base_ptr[handle.extent_index()] + handle.offset_bytes(). The sentinel pattern (MAX, MAX-1, MAX-2) maps to GPU's LoserTree sentinel concept. Using __clz for fast free-list indexing by size class mirrors CrocSort's FREE_LISTS array.
ideas.novelty: CrocSort's specific handle encoding adapted for GPU smem addressing is novel.
related_work: Steinberger et al., ScatterAlloc, InPar 2012
NVIDIA, Device Memory Allocation, 2023
ideas.experiment: Measure handle encode/decode throughput and allocator operations per second on GPU vs CPU implementation.

ideas.title: Late Fence Slots as Warp-Level Bitmask for Run Boundary Tracking
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's LateFenceSlots (tracking exhausted tree positions) to a warp-wide bitmask using ballot operations.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/replacement_selection/late_fence_slots.rs:LateFenceSlots — tracks which loser tree slots are exhausted
ideas.crocsort_concept: Late fence slots: bitmask tracking which tree positions hold LateFence sentinels; used for run switching
ideas.gpu_primitive: __ballot_sync for bitmask, __popc for counting exhausted slots, __ffs for finding first available
ideas.detail: CrocSort's LateFenceSlots tracks which loser tree positions are exhausted (holding LateFence sentinel). On GPU with tree capacity <= 32 (fits in one warp), the entire late fence state is a single 32-bit bitmask computed by __ballot_sync. __popc counts exhausted slots (for run switch decisions), __ffs finds the first available slot. This replaces CrocSort's Vec<bool> with a single register.
ideas.novelty: Warp ballot as late fence tracking for loser tree is a GPU-specific optimization of CrocSort's replacement selection.
related_work: Fung et al., Dynamic Warp Formation, MICRO 2007
Harris, GPU Computing Gems, 2010
ideas.experiment: Measure replacement selection throughput with bitmask vs array-based late fence tracking on GPU.

ideas.title: GPU Extent-Based Memory with HBM Huge Pages
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's 64KB extent model to GPU HBM using 2MB huge pages, reducing TLB pressure during run generation and merge.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/replacement_selection/memory.rs:EXTENT_SIZE = DEFAULT_BUFFER_SIZE (64KB); add_extent allocates new chunks
ideas.crocsort_concept: Extent-based allocation: 64KB chunks, new extent when current is full
ideas.gpu_primitive: cudaMallocManaged with CU_MEM_ALLOC_GRANULARITY_RECOMMENDED, huge page support
ideas.detail: CrocSort allocates memory in 64KB extents. On GPU, use 2MB huge pages (cudaMallocManaged with appropriate flags) as the extent size. This reduces TLB misses during sort's streaming access pattern. Each extent holds ~2MB/record_size records. The MemoryManager's extent vector maps to an array of huge-page-aligned HBM allocations.
ideas.novelty: GPU sorts don't consider TLB pressure from extent allocation. Huge page-aligned extent model is novel.
related_work: Basu et al., Efficient Virtual Memory for Big Data, ISCA 2013
Ausavarungnirun et al., Mosaic: GPU TLB, ISCA 2017
ideas.experiment: Measure TLB miss rate and sort throughput with 4KB vs 2MB extent granularity on A100.

ideas.title: Free-List Indexing by Size Class via GPU Warp-Level __clz
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's free-list indexing (FREE_LISTS array indexed by size/ALIGN) to GPU using __clz for O(1) size class lookup.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/replacement_selection/memory.rs:FREE_LISTS = EXTENT_SIZE/ALIGN (line 7); find_free_block scans from required size class upward
ideas.crocsort_concept: Free-list indexed by 32B size class; find_free_block scans from required class up; last_non_null optimization
ideas.gpu_primitive: __clz (count leading zeros) for size class computation, shared memory free-list array
ideas.detail: CrocSort indexes free-lists by size/ALIGN. On GPU, compute size class = __clz(size) for O(1) power-of-2 class lookup. The last_non_null optimization (tracking highest non-empty free-list) maps to a shared-memory variable updated atomically. This enables the best-fit allocator's find_free_block to run in O(1) average case on GPU.
ideas.novelty: GPU allocators typically use hash-based or slab allocation. Free-list with __clz indexing matching CrocSort's scheme is novel.
related_work: Steinberger et al., ScatterAlloc, InPar 2012
Winter et al., faimGraph: GPU Dynamic Graph, Euro-Par 2018
ideas.experiment: Measure alloc/free throughput with free-list vs slab allocation for variable-size records on GPU.

ideas.title: Run Sink as GPU Async Writer with Circular HBM Buffer
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's RunSink trait (start_run/push_record/finish_run) to a GPU async writer using circular HBM buffers with cp.async.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/run_sink.rs:RunSink trait — start_run, push_record, finish_run, finalize
ideas.crocsort_concept: RunSink: streaming output interface for run generation; decouples sort from I/O
ideas.gpu_primitive: cp.async for smem-to-HBM writes, circular buffer with producer/consumer indices
ideas.detail: CrocSort's RunSink provides a streaming interface for writing sorted records to runs. On GPU, implement as a circular HBM buffer: replacement selection writes to smem, cp.async copies from smem to HBM circular buffer. The start_run/finish_run boundaries map to buffer position markers. Multiple SMs write to the same circular buffer using atomic producer index advancement. Consumer (merge or output) reads from the tail.
ideas.novelty: GPU sort output is typically synchronous. Async circular buffer matching CrocSort's RunSink streaming model is novel.
related_work: Tzeng et al., Task Management for Irregular GPU Workloads, HPCA 2010
NVIDIA, cp.async Best Practices, 2022
ideas.experiment: Measure run generation throughput with synchronous vs async circular buffer output on A100.

ideas.title: Coalescing Block Merge via GPU Warp-Level Memory Operations
ideas.summary: Port CrocSort's MemoryManager block coalescing (left/right neighbor merge) to GPU using warp-level shared memory operations.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/replacement_selection/memory.rs:MemoryManager::free (line 165) — coalesces left and right neighbors
ideas.crocsort_concept: Block coalescing: check left footer, check right header; merge free blocks; prevents fragmentation
ideas.gpu_primitive: Shared memory read/write with __syncwarp for fence consistency
ideas.detail: CrocSort's free() checks the left neighbor (via footer at offset-4) and right neighbor (via header at offset+size) for coalescing. On GPU shared memory, these are pointer-arithmetic reads. __syncwarp ensures memory consistency between free/alloc operations within a warp. The coalescing prevents fragmentation in the smem arena, maintaining high utilization (rho) for replacement selection.
ideas.novelty: GPU shared memory allocators rarely implement block coalescing. Porting CrocSort's coalescing logic is novel.
related_work: Steinberger et al., ScatterAlloc, InPar 2012
Knuth, The Art of Computer Programming Vol 1, 1968
ideas.experiment: Measure fragmentation rate (free space / total - in_use) with and without coalescing during GPU replacement selection.

ideas.title: GPU Sort Output Materialization vs Discard Mode
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's discard_final_output mode (EmptySortOutput) to GPU, enabling benchmark-only execution that skips final HBM writes.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:SorterCore::set_discard_final_output (line 280); lib.rs:EmptySortOutput
ideas.crocsort_concept: Discard output mode for benchmarking; sort executes but final materialization skipped
ideas.gpu_primitive: Predicated writes (suppress HBM writes via flag), kernel parameter for discard mode
ideas.detail: CrocSort's discard mode (EmptySortOutput) executes the full sort but skips final output, used for benchmarking to isolate sort performance from I/O. On GPU, pass a discard flag to the final merge kernel that suppresses HBM writes via predication. This enables accurate benchmarking of sort compute + intermediate I/O without final materialization cost. ZeroCopyMergeWithOVC::discard (merge.rs:113) already implements this pattern.
ideas.novelty: GPU sort benchmarks always materialize output. A discard mode matching CrocSort's design enables isolated performance measurement.
related_work: Stehle & Jacobsen, GPU External Sort, SIGMOD 2017
NVIDIA, NSight Compute Profiling, 2023
ideas.experiment: Compare measured sort throughput with and without final output materialization on GenSort 80GB to quantify I/O overhead.

ideas.title: GPU AlignedWriter as Coalesced HBM Burst Writer
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's AlignedWriter (direct I/O with aligned buffers) to GPU coalesced HBM writes with 128B alignment for maximum bandwidth.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/diskio/aligned_writer.rs:AlignedWriter — writes aligned to DEFAULT_BUFFER_SIZE; pwrite_fd for positioned writes
ideas.crocsort_concept: AlignedWriter: buffered writes aligned to page boundary; flush on buffer full
ideas.gpu_primitive: 128B-aligned HBM writes, vectorized stores (float4/int4 for 16B coalesced writes)
ideas.detail: CrocSort's AlignedWriter buffers data and writes in aligned chunks. On GPU, align all run output writes to 128B HBM cache lines. Use vectorized stores (int4 = 16B per thread, 32 threads per warp = 512B coalesced write). The DEFAULT_BUFFER_SIZE alignment from CrocSort maps to 128B GPU cache line alignment. Partial cache lines at run boundaries use masked stores to avoid read-modify-write overhead.
ideas.novelty: GPU sorts write records individually (uncoalesced for variable-size records). CrocSort's buffered aligned write model adapted for GPU coalesced access is novel.
related_work: Harris, CUDA Memory Coalescing Best Practices, 2012
NVIDIA, Global Memory Coalescing Guide, 2023
ideas.experiment: Measure HBM write bandwidth with aligned coalesced vs unaligned individual writes during merge output.

ideas.title: GPU AlignedReader as Prefetch-Ahead HBM Burst Reader
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's AlignedReader/AlignedChunkReader to GPU with cp.async prefetch-ahead pattern for merge input streams.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/diskio/aligned_reader.rs:AlignedReader — buffered reads from file descriptor; sequential scan pattern
ideas.crocsort_concept: AlignedReader: aligned read buffer; sequential scan; used by merge source scan_range
ideas.gpu_primitive: cp.async.ca (cache-all) for prefetch, double-buffer with cp.async.wait_group
ideas.detail: CrocSort's AlignedReader reads data in aligned chunks. On GPU, implement as double-buffered cp.async: while processing current buffer (smem buffer A), prefetch next chunk from HBM (into smem buffer B). The scan_range_with_io_tracker pattern from run.rs maps to sequential HBM reads with stride. cp.async.ca hint tells the cache controller to cache the prefetched data, improving L2 hit rate for subsequent accesses.
ideas.novelty: GPU merge reads are typically demand-driven. Prefetch-ahead pattern matching CrocSort's AlignedReader streaming model is novel.
related_work: Dao et al., FlashAttention, NeurIPS 2022
NVIDIA, Asynchronous Copy Best Practices, 2022
ideas.experiment: Measure merge input stream bandwidth with demand-fetch vs prefetch-ahead on varying record sizes.

ideas.title: Zero-Copy Merge Value Streaming on GPU via DMA Engine
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's ZeroCopyMergeWithOVC (values flow reader->writer without buffering) to GPU using copy engine for direct HBM-to-HBM value transfer.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/ovc/merge.rs:ZeroCopyMergeWithOVC (line 79) — keys in tree, values streamed via copy_value_to
ideas.crocsort_concept: Zero-copy merge: keys live in loser tree, values never buffered — copied directly source->output
ideas.gpu_primitive: cudaMemcpyAsync (DMA copy engine), concurrent copy + compute streams
ideas.detail: CrocSort's ZeroCopyMerge keeps only keys in the loser tree; values are copied directly from source reader to output writer via copy_value_to (merge.rs:176). On GPU, the copy engine can transfer values between HBM locations independently of the SMs. While SMs process the loser tree (keys), the copy engine transfers winner values. This mirrors CrocSort's CPU pattern where I/O and comparison overlap. Use separate CUDA streams for compute (key comparison) and copy (value transfer).
ideas.novelty: GPU merges always load full records into SM. Decoupling key comparison (SM) from value transfer (copy engine) matching CrocSort's zero-copy design is novel.
related_work: Stehle & Jacobsen, GPU External Sort, SIGMOD 2017
NVIDIA, Concurrent Copy and Compute, 2023
ideas.experiment: Measure merge throughput with SM-only vs SM+copy engine on varying key:value size ratios (1:1, 1:10, 1:100).
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs Stehle: loads full records to SM. Gap vs NVIDIA guide: general concurrent copy, not applied to merge sort. Risk: copy engine bandwidth is shared with host-device transfers. If multiple merge threads use the copy engine simultaneously, they contend for bandwidth. Mitigation: batch value copies — accumulate several winner values then issue one large cudaMemcpyAsync. CrocSort's AlignedWriter buffering (aligned_writer.rs) provides the same batching on CPU. Eq.5 analogue: with zero-copy, merge compute cost = key_comparison_only. Value transfer cost = value_bytes / copy_engine_bandwidth. Total = max(compute, transfer) instead of compute + transfer. For large values (10x key), this 2x improvement.
ideas.experiment_plan: Baseline: Standard GPU merge loading full records, ModernGPU merge, CUB merge. Metrics: (1) effective merge throughput in GB/s (total data / time), (2) SM utilization % during merge (should be higher when values offloaded to DMA), (3) copy engine utilization % (NSight Compute). Datasets: GenSort 80GB (key=10B, value=90B, ratio 1:9), lineitem-SF100 (key=20B, value=~150B, ratio 1:7.5), HeavyKey 40GB (extreme value sizes). Milestones: M1 — loser tree merge kernel operating on keys only; M2 — DMA value transfer integrated with separate CUDA stream; M3 — batched value copy with AlignedWriter-style buffering. Validation: verify that output data matches standard merge. SM utilization should increase from ~40% to ~80% when values are offloaded. Expected: 2-3x throughput improvement on value-heavy datasets (value > 10x key size).

ideas.title: IoStatsTracker as GPU Performance Counter Aggregator
ideas.summary: Port CrocSort's IoStatsTracker (per-thread I/O statistics) to GPU using per-SM atomic counters for read/write byte tracking.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/diskio/io_stats.rs:IoStatsTracker — atomic read_bytes, write_bytes, read_ops, write_ops counters
ideas.crocsort_concept: IoStatsTracker: per-thread atomic I/O accounting; aggregated post-sort for read amplification
ideas.gpu_primitive: atomicAdd for per-SM counters, __threadfence for visibility across SMs
ideas.detail: CrocSort tracks I/O statistics atomically per thread for post-sort analysis (read amplification, per-thread I/O imbalance). On GPU, use per-SM atomic counters in global memory. Each merge thread block atomics its read/write bytes. Post-merge, aggregate across SMs on host. This enables the same observability metrics (read_amplification, io_imbalance) on GPU that CrocSort provides on CPU.
ideas.novelty: GPU sorts lack CrocSort-level I/O observability. Porting IoStatsTracker to GPU enables principled performance analysis.
related_work: NVIDIA, NSight Compute User Guide, 2023
Stehle & Jacobsen, GPU Sort, SIGMOD 2017
ideas.experiment: Measure read amplification and per-SM I/O imbalance on GPU sort, comparing with CrocSort's CPU measurements.

ideas.title: GPU Direct I/O for External Sort Beyond HBM
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's direct I/O (O_DIRECT in aligned_reader.rs) to GPU Direct Storage (GDS) for external sort that spills beyond HBM to NVMe SSDs.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/diskio/aligned_reader.rs:AlignedReader; src/diskio/file.rs:SharedFd; feature flag buffered_io
ideas.crocsort_concept: Direct I/O: aligned reads/writes bypassing OS page cache; feature flag for buffered fallback
ideas.gpu_primitive: GPU Direct Storage (GDS / cuFile), GPUDirect RDMA for NVMe access
ideas.detail: CrocSort uses O_DIRECT for aligned I/O bypassing the OS page cache. On GPU, GDS enables direct NVMe-to-GPU-HBM transfers bypassing CPU and system memory. For datasets exceeding HBM (>80GB on A100), runs spill to NVMe and are read back during merge via GDS. CrocSort's AlignedReader/Writer pattern maps directly to cuFile read/write with alignment requirements. The buffered_io feature flag maps to a fallback using cudaMemcpy through host memory.
ideas.novelty: GDS for GPU external merge sort is underexplored. CrocSort's direct I/O architecture provides the right abstraction layer for GPU adaptation.
related_work: NVIDIA, GPUDirect Storage Documentation, 2023
Stehle & Jacobsen, GPU External Sort, SIGMOD 2017
ideas.experiment: Compare sort throughput for 200GB dataset on A100: GDS (NVMe->GPU) vs host-staged (NVMe->CPU->GPU) data path.

ideas.title: Multi-GPU NVLink Sort with CrocSort M/D Transfer Property
ideas.summary: CrocSort's scale transfer property (top configs transfer across D scales) predicts that optimal GPU sort config found on 1 GPU transfers to 8 GPUs via NVLink.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:SorterCore config; cloud paper: top-3 configs at SF50 within 5% at SF200
ideas.crocsort_concept: Scale transfer: M/D ratio governs surface shape; optimal configs transfer across D scales
ideas.gpu_primitive: NCCL AllToAll for data redistribution, NVLink for inter-GPU communication
ideas.detail: The cloud paper shows top-3 CPU configs at SF50 stay within 5% gap at SF200. On multi-GPU, total M = sum(per_GPU_SMEM) and total D = sum(per_GPU_HBM_data). The M/D ratio stays constant when scaling from 1 to N GPUs (both M and D scale linearly). This predicts that optimal per-GPU sort config transfers directly to multi-GPU — a powerful result for auto-tuning. Use NCCL AllToAll for the data redistribution phase between per-GPU run generation and global merge.
ideas.novelty: No multi-GPU sort study leverages CrocSort's transfer property. Using M/D ratio invariance for multi-GPU auto-tuning is novel.
related_work: Rui et al., Multi-GPU Sort, TPDS 2021
Tanasic et al., GPU Sorting Networks, PPoPP 2014
ideas.experiment: Validate transfer property: find optimal config on 1 GPU, test on 2/4/8 GPUs, measure performance gap.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs Rui: multi-GPU sort but no analytical model for config transfer. Gap vs Tanasic: sorting networks, not merge sort. Risk: NVLink AllToAll during redistribution adds latency not present in single-GPU sort. The cloud paper's latency sensitivity finding (100us -> 2.5x wider gap) predicts NVLink latency (~0.5us) will have minimal impact, but PCIe (~5us) could be significant. Eq.5 multi-GPU: M = N * SMEM_per_GPU, D = N * D_per_GPU, P = NVLink_message_size (typically 128B-4KB). The M/D ratio = SMEM/HBM is invariant under N-scaling, confirming transfer.
ideas.experiment_plan: Baseline: Single-GPU CUB sort, NCCL-based naive sort (sort locally, merge globally). Metrics: (1) end-to-end sort throughput in GB/s across 1/2/4/8 GPUs, (2) config sensitivity (throughput variance across top-5 configs), (3) NVLink bandwidth utilization %. Datasets: GenSort scaled to fill: 80GB (1 GPU), 160GB (2 GPUs), 640GB (8 GPUs). Milestones: M1 — single-GPU sort with transfer-verified optimal config; M2 — NCCL-based data redistribution; M3 — full multi-GPU sort with config transfer validation. Validation: top-3 configs on 1 GPU should be within 5% of optimal on 8 GPUs (matching CrocSort's transfer property). Expected: config transfer holds, enabling 1-GPU tuning for N-GPU deployment. 8-GPU sort achieves near-linear speedup (7.5x) on 640GB.

ideas.title: Skew Handling on SIMD Lanes via OVC-Guided Load Balancing
ideas.summary: CrocSort's HeavyKey handling (byte-balanced partitioning for 256x payload skew) maps to SIMD lane load balancing where OVC guides per-lane work assignment.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/run_format.rs:select_boundary_by_size; lib.rs:MergeStats byte imbalance tracking
ideas.crocsort_concept: Byte-balanced vs count-balanced: identical for uniform, critical for 256x skew
ideas.gpu_primitive: Warp-level work redistribution via __shfl_sync, per-lane byte counting
ideas.detail: In a warp-level merge, each lane processes one merge source. With HeavyKey-style skew (some values 256x larger), lanes with large-value sources finish later, causing warp divergence. Solution: use OVC offset field to estimate remaining work per lane, then redistribute via __shfl_sync. Lanes with less remaining work steal from heavy lanes. This is CrocSort's byte-balanced partitioning at the warp level rather than the thread level.
ideas.novelty: Warp-level byte-balanced load balancing for skewed merge is novel. GPU merges assume uniform work per lane.
related_work: Tzeng et al., Task Management for Irregular Workloads, HPCA 2010
Davidson et al., Work-Efficient Parallel GPU Merge, PPoPP 2012
ideas.experiment: Measure warp utilization on HeavyKey dataset with uniform vs byte-balanced lane assignment.

ideas.title: Cloud Latency Findings Applied to GPU-PCIe-NVMe Memory Hierarchy
ideas.summary: CrocSort's storage latency findings (100us/500us sensitivity) predict behavior of GPU sort across the HBM→PCIe→NVMe hierarchy for external sort.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:SorterCore; cloud paper: 100us->2.5x gap, 500us->fewer threads optimal
ideas.crocsort_concept: Storage-aware I/O term; latency sensitivity; optimal config shifts with latency
ideas.gpu_primitive: GPU Direct Storage latency profiling, cudaEventElapsedTime for I/O latency measurement
ideas.detail: GPU memory hierarchy: HBM (~100ns), PCIe (~5us), NVMe via GDS (~10-50us). CrocSort's finding that 500us latency shifts optimal to fewer threads predicts that NVMe-backed GPU sort should use fewer merge streams than HBM-only sort. At ~50us GDS latency (vs ~100ns HBM), the optimal merge fanin may drop from 64 to 8-16, matching the cloud paper's prediction scaled to GPU latencies.
ideas.novelty: No GPU external sort models latency sensitivity across the HBM/PCIe/NVMe stack using CrocSort's analytical framework.
related_work: NVIDIA, GPU Direct Storage Performance Guide, 2023
Rui et al., Multi-GPU Sort, TPDS 2021
ideas.experiment: Sweep merge fanin on A100 with data in HBM, PCIe-attached host memory, and NVMe-backed GDS, measuring throughput vs latency.

ideas.title: GenSort vs Lineitem on GPU: Narrow vs Broad Optimal Surface
ideas.summary: CrocSort's finding that GenSort has a narrow sharp optimal ridge while adversarial distributions are broad+flat predicts GPU sort config sensitivity per workload.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:SorterCore config space; cloud paper: uniform narrow vs adversarial broad
ideas.crocsort_concept: Uniform (GenSort): narrow sharp optimal ridge. Adversarial (200B prefix keys): broad flat surface
ideas.gpu_primitive: CUDA autotuning via cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialBlockSize sweep
ideas.detail: The cloud paper shows GenSort (uniform records) has a narrow performance ridge — small config changes hurt performance. Adversarial distributions (long common prefixes) have a broad flat surface — many configs work well. On GPU, this predicts: GenSort requires precise block size / smem configuration, while lineitem (prefix-heavy) is robust to config variation. This guides GPU sort auto-tuning: spend more tuning effort on uniform workloads.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort auto-tuner accounts for workload-dependent performance surface shape.
related_work: Ansel et al., OpenTuner, PACT 2014
Nugteren & Codreanu, CLTune, ACM TACO 2015
ideas.experiment: Map GPU sort performance surface for GenSort vs lineitem across 100 configurations, measuring ridge width.

ideas.title: CrocSort Thread Pool Mapping to GPU Persistent Thread Model
ideas.summary: Map CrocSort's thread pool architecture (run-gen threads + merge threads with barrier sync) to GPU persistent threads for the entire sort lifecycle.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:execute_run_generation (thread::spawn) + multi_merge (thread scoped)
ideas.crocsort_concept: Thread pool: run-gen threads produce runs, barrier, merge threads consume; SorterCore orchestrates
ideas.gpu_primitive: Persistent threads (grid-level loop), cooperative_groups::grid_group::sync()
ideas.detail: CrocSort spawns run-gen threads, collects results, then spawns merge threads. On GPU, a single persistent kernel can play both roles: initial grid loops over input data for run generation, then cg::grid_group::sync() acts as the barrier, then the same grid loops over runs for merge. No kernel relaunch overhead. The SorterCore's orchestration logic moves from host to device, with only configuration remaining on host.
ideas.novelty: GPU sorts launch separate kernels per phase. A persistent thread model matching CrocSort's unified thread pool is novel.
related_work: Gupta et al., Persistent Threads, ISCA 2012
NVIDIA, Cooperative Groups, 2023
ideas.experiment: Compare persistent-thread sort vs multi-kernel sort, measuring kernel launch overhead and total sort time.

ideas.title: TMA-Accelerated Run Generation on H100
ideas.summary: Use H100's Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA) for bulk loading input records to shared memory during run generation, replacing explicit cp.async loops.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/replacement_selection/tol_mm.rs:run_replacement_selection_mm — loads records from scanner into tree workspace
ideas.crocsort_concept: Run generation: load records -> insert into loser tree -> emit sorted output
ideas.gpu_primitive: TMA (Tensor Memory Accelerator) on H100, cp.async.bulk for large transfers
ideas.detail: H100's TMA can transfer multi-dimensional tensor tiles directly from HBM to shared memory in a single instruction, replacing loops of cp.async calls. For run generation, the input record batch is a 1D tensor (N records × record_size). TMA loads the entire batch (up to 128KB) in one instruction, then the SM processes the batch for loser tree insertion. This reduces instruction count and memory controller overhead vs explicit copy loops.
ideas.novelty: TMA for sort data loading is entirely unexplored. Mapping CrocSort's batch record loading to TMA is novel.
related_work: Dao et al., FlashAttention-2, 2023
NVIDIA, H100 Whitepaper, 2022
ideas.experiment: Compare TMA vs cp.async for record batch loading during run generation on H100, measuring load latency and throughput.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs FlashAttention-2: uses TMA for matrix tiles, not sort records. Gap vs H100 whitepaper: describes TMA for ML workloads, not database sort. Risk: TMA requires tensor descriptors to be set up on host before kernel launch. For variable-size records, descriptors must be pre-computed or use the maximum record size (wasting bandwidth for small records). Mitigation: use TMA for fixed-size records (GenSort 100B), fall back to cp.async for variable-size. Eq.5 analogue: TMA reduces the 'load' component of run generation time. CrocSort's RunGenerationStats tracks load_time_ms separately — TMA should reduce this by 2-3x on H100.

ideas.title: Cluster-Level Replacement Selection on H100 SM Clusters
ideas.summary: Use H100's SM cluster feature (groups of 4 SMs sharing L1 cache) for replacement selection with a larger combined workspace.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/replacement_selection/tol_mm.rs:ReplacementSelectionMM — loser tree in memory manager with workspace limit
ideas.crocsort_concept: Replacement selection workspace size determines run length; larger workspace -> longer runs -> fewer merge levels
ideas.gpu_primitive: H100 SM clusters (cudaClusterLaunch), distributed shared memory across cluster
ideas.detail: H100 clusters group 4 SMs that can access each other's shared memory. Total smem per cluster: 4 * 228KB = 912KB. This 4x larger workspace enables ~4x longer runs (higher T*), reducing merge levels. The loser tree resides in one SM's smem, while record buffers span all 4 SMs. Cross-SM access within cluster has ~5 cycle latency (vs ~200 for HBM), enabling efficient large-workspace replacement selection.
ideas.novelty: SM clusters for sort workloads are entirely unexplored. Using distributed shared memory for enlarged replacement selection workspace is novel.
related_work: NVIDIA, H100 Architecture Whitepaper, 2022
NVIDIA, CUDA Thread Block Clusters, 2023
ideas.experiment: Compare run length and expansion factor E with 1-SM vs 4-SM cluster replacement selection on H100.
ideas.deepen_notes: Gap vs NVIDIA: SM clusters documented for ML workloads, not sort. Risk: cross-SM shared memory access within cluster adds ~5 cycle latency vs ~1 cycle for local smem. This means the loser tree (frequent access) should stay in local smem, while record buffer (less frequent per-record access) can span cluster. CrocSort's L* formula adapts: L_local* for local tree, remaining across cluster for records. Eq.5 analogue: M = 912KB (cluster smem), T* = 0.7 * 912KB * 1.41 / sqrt(80GB * 128B) = ~0.28. Still < 1, but 4x better than single SM. With 30 clusters on H100: T* ≈ 30 * 0.28 = 8.4 pooled runs — approaching useful territory.
ideas.experiment_plan: Baseline: Single-SM replacement selection (228KB workspace), standard GPU sort run generation. Metrics: (1) average run length in records, (2) expansion factor E = avg_run_length / workspace_capacity, (3) total runs generated for 80GB dataset. Datasets: GenSort 80GB (random -> E≈2), pre-sorted GenSort (E>>2), lineitem-SF100. Milestones: M1 — basic cluster launch with distributed smem; M2 — cross-SM record buffer access working; M3 — full replacement selection with cluster workspace. Validation: E should be ~2 on random data (matching CrocSort CPU), higher on pre-sorted (matching CrocSort's observation). Expected: 4x longer runs = log4(fewer_runs) fewer merge levels = measurable total sort speedup.

ideas.title: H100 Asynchronous Barrier for Zero-Overhead Phase Transition
ideas.summary: Use H100's hardware asynchronous barriers for the run-gen → merge phase transition, replacing software sync.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:run_generation then multi_merge — sequential phases with implicit barrier
ideas.crocsort_concept: Phase transition: all run-gen threads complete before merge begins
ideas.gpu_primitive: cuda::barrier (hardware async barrier), mbarrier instructions
ideas.detail: CrocSort has an implicit barrier between run generation and merge (run_generation returns, then multi_merge is called). On GPU with persistent kernels, use H100's hardware async barriers (mbarrier) for zero-overhead phase transition. The hardware barrier avoids software polling overhead and enables precise synchronization between SM clusters finishing run generation at different times.
ideas.novelty: Hardware async barriers for sort phase transition is novel.
related_work: NVIDIA, CUDA Async Barriers, 2023
Gupta et al., Persistent Threads, ISCA 2012
ideas.experiment: Measure phase transition overhead with software barrier vs hardware mbarrier on H100.

ideas.title: FP8 OVC Encoding for Tensor Core Merge on H100
ideas.summary: Encode OVC values as FP8 (E4M3) for H100 Tensor Core-accelerated comparison operations during merge.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/ovc/offset_value_coding_32.rs:OVCU32 — 32-bit integer OVC
ideas.crocsort_concept: OVC comparison: integer compare on packed 32-bit value determines sort order
ideas.gpu_primitive: H100 FP8 Tensor Cores, __nv_fp8_e4m3 type, wmma with FP8 precision
ideas.detail: H100 Tensor Cores support FP8 operations at 2x throughput vs FP16. Encode OVC's 16-bit value field as two FP8 values (offset as E4M3, value as E4M3). Use wmma operations to compare multiple OVC values simultaneously. For a 16-way merge, encode 16 OVC values in a matrix and use FP8 matmul to compute the comparison matrix in one Tensor Core instruction. 2x faster than FP16 Tensor Core approach (cycle5_idea5).
ideas.novelty: FP8 Tensor Cores for sort comparison is entirely unexplored.
related_work: Micikevicius et al., FP8 Formats for Deep Learning, 2022
Dakkak et al., Tensor Core Reduction, ICS 2019
ideas.experiment: Compare FP8 Tensor Core OVC merge vs FP16 vs INT32 scalar merge throughput on H100.

ideas.title: Thread Block Cluster Merge with Distributed Loser Tree
ideas.summary: Distribute CrocSort's loser tree across an H100 SM cluster, with each SM holding a subtree and cross-SM communication for tournament replay.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/ovc/tree_of_losers.rs:LoserTree — tree_of_losers with pass() tournament replay
ideas.crocsort_concept: Loser tree: k-way merge via tournament; Phase 1 climb, Phase 2 bubble-up with geometric subtree check
ideas.gpu_primitive: H100 distributed shared memory, cross-SM loads within cluster
ideas.detail: For high fanin merge (K=256), a single SM's smem can't hold the entire loser tree + K read buffers. With H100 clusters, distribute: SM0 holds tree levels 0-3 (top 16 nodes), SMs 1-3 hold subtrees of 64 nodes each, plus read buffers for their 64 sources. The tournament replay crosses SM boundaries only for the top levels (levels 0-3), keeping most access local. CrocSort's geometric subtree property (tree_of_losers.rs:806) determines which SM owns which subtree.
ideas.novelty: Distributed loser tree across SM cluster is entirely novel. Uses CrocSort's geometric property for SM assignment.
related_work: Agrawal et al., Scalable Sorting on Distributed Memory, PODC 2018
NVIDIA, H100 Cluster, 2022
ideas.experiment: Compare K=256 merge with distributed cluster tree vs K=64 with single-SM tree (requiring multi-pass).

ideas.title: End-to-End GPU CrocSort: Unified Architecture Combining All Transfers
ideas.summary: Combine the best ideas into a unified GPU sort architecture: OVC in registers, sparse indexes in L2, replacement selection in SM clusters, byte-balanced partitioning, zero-copy merge with DMA.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/ovc/sorter.rs:ExternalSorterWithOVC — full pipeline: run-gen -> boundary selection -> merge
ideas.crocsort_concept: Full CrocSort pipeline: replacement selection run-gen -> sparse index -> byte-balanced partitioning -> OVC merge
ideas.gpu_primitive: All: cooperative_groups, cp.async, TMA, SM clusters, L2 persistence, __shfl_sync, DMA copy engine
ideas.detail: The unified architecture: (1) SM cluster replacement selection with OVC (cycle 14) generates runs with sparse indexes (cycle 7). (2) Sparse indexes pinned in L2 (cycle 2) enable parallel byte-balanced boundary search (cycle 7). (3) OVC-enhanced zero-copy merge (cycles 4,5,12) with register-resident loser tree reads only keys on OVC tie-break. (4) Analytical model (cycle 1) determines SM allocation between phases. This is the full CrocSort-on-GPU system.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort combines all these techniques. The unified architecture is novel as a system, not just individual components.
related_work: All prior work from cycles 1-14
CrocSort VLDB 2026 paper
ideas.experiment: End-to-end comparison of unified GPU CrocSort vs CUB, Thrust, ModernGPU on GenSort/lineitem/HeavyKey at 80-640GB scale.
ideas.deepen_notes: This is the capstone idea combining all previous work. Gap vs all prior GPU sort: no existing system uses OVC, sparse indexes, byte-balanced partitioning, or analytical resource allocation. Risk: integration complexity — each component has been sketched independently, but interactions (e.g., OVC register pressure affecting replacement selection occupancy) need careful co-optimization. Implementation order: start with OVC merge (most impactful single component), then add sparse indexes, then replacement selection, then analytical tuning. Eq.5 analogue: full GPU equation: T_sort = max(T_gen, T_merge) where T_gen = D/(N_SM * E * SMEM_cluster * throughput_per_SM) and T_merge = 2D * (1+L_avg) / min(HBM_BW, N_SM * merge_throughput_per_SM).
ideas.experiment_plan: Baseline: CUB DeviceMergeSort, Thrust sort_by_key, ModernGPU mergesort, Stehle GPU external sort, CPU CrocSort. Metrics: (1) end-to-end sort throughput in GB/s, (2) HBM bandwidth efficiency (achieved/peak), (3) read amplification, (4) merge thread byte imbalance factor, (5) OVC hit rate %. Datasets: GenSort 80GB/640GB, lineitem-SF100/SF500, FreqKey 40GB/320GB, HeavyKey 40GB/320GB. Milestones: M1 — OVC merge kernel beating CUB on lineitem; M2 — sparse index + byte-balanced partitioning reducing imbalance on HeavyKey; M3 — full pipeline with cluster replacement selection; M4 — multi-GPU scaling to 640GB. Validation: GPU sort should match CrocSort CPU sort order. Read amplification should be <1.05x (matching CPU CrocSort). Byte imbalance should be <1.1x on HeavyKey. Expected: 5-10x over CUB on lineitem (OVC benefit), 2-3x on GenSort (pipeline efficiency), 3-5x on HeavyKey (byte-balanced partitioning). Near-linear multi-GPU scaling.

ideas.title: Adaptive GPU Sort Selection via CrocSort Cost Model
ideas.summary: Use CrocSort's analytical model to automatically choose between GPU radix sort, merge sort, and CrocSort-style sort based on data characteristics.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/sort/core/engine.rs:SorterCore — parameterized by run_gen_threads, merge_threads, etc.
ideas.crocsort_concept: Analytical model predicts optimal config; M/D ratio and data characteristics determine best approach
ideas.gpu_primitive: Runtime decision kernel, CUB DeviceRadixSort vs custom merge sort dispatch
ideas.detail: For small data (fits in HBM): use CUB DeviceRadixSort (fastest for in-memory). For medium data (2-10x HBM): use CrocSort-GPU with analytical model for config. For large data (>10x HBM): use CrocSort-GPU with GDS. For high prefix redundancy: always enable OVC. The decision tree uses CrocSort's M/D ratio and E prediction to choose at runtime.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort auto-selects algorithm based on an analytical cost model. CrocSort's model as a GPU sort dispatcher is novel.
related_work: Ansel et al., OpenTuner, PACT 2014
Bandle et al., To Partition or Not to Partition, VLDB 2021
ideas.experiment: Compare auto-selected vs fixed algorithm across 10 diverse datasets, measuring geometric mean throughput.

ideas.title: GPU CrocSort as Database Operator for GPU Query Processing
ideas.summary: Integrate GPU CrocSort as a sort operator in GPU database engines (e.g., BlazingSQL, RAPIDS cuDF), replacing their built-in sort.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/lib.rs:Sorter trait, SortInput/SortOutput traits — clean operator interface
ideas.crocsort_concept: Sorter trait: sort(SortInput) -> SortOutput; clean interface for database integration
ideas.gpu_primitive: RAPIDS cuDF column format, Apache Arrow GPU memory format, custom CUDA sort kernel
ideas.detail: CrocSort's Sorter trait provides a clean interface: sort(SortInput) -> SortOutput. On GPU, SortInput = cuDF DataFrame column, SortOutput = sorted column with stats. The OVC encoding benefits compound key sorts (ORDER BY col1, col2) common in SQL. Byte-balanced partitioning handles join-produced skewed intermediate data. Integration with RAPIDS enables drop-in replacement for cuDF's current sort.
ideas.novelty: No GPU database uses CrocSort's techniques. Integrating OVC+sparse indexes as a GPU database sort operator is novel.
related_work: Shanbhag et al., Crystal: GPU-Optimized SQL, SIGMOD 2020
NVIDIA RAPIDS cuDF Documentation, 2023
ideas.experiment: Compare GPU CrocSort sort operator vs cuDF sort on TPC-H queries requiring ORDER BY, measuring query execution time.

ideas.title: GPU CrocSort Profiling Framework Matching CPU Observability
ideas.summary: Build a GPU profiling framework that produces the same metrics as CrocSort's SortStats, enabling direct CPU vs GPU performance comparison.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/lib.rs:SortStats, RunGenerationStats, MergeStats — comprehensive sort performance metrics
ideas.crocsort_concept: SortStats: run count, per-phase timing, I/O stats, thread imbalance, read amplification
ideas.gpu_primitive: CUDA events for timing, NSight Compute counters for I/O, per-block atomic counters
ideas.detail: CrocSort's SortStats provides: num_runs, run_gen time, merge time, per-thread timing, read amplification, byte imbalance. Build an equivalent GPU framework: CUDA events per kernel for timing, atomic counters per thread block for entry/byte counts, NSight Compute integration for HBM traffic. Output format matches SortStats exactly, enabling direct comparison with CPU CrocSort runs.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort framework provides CrocSort-level observability. A matching metric framework enables principled CPU vs GPU comparison.
related_work: CrocSort VLDB 2026 paper
NVIDIA NSight Compute, 2023
ideas.experiment: Run identical workloads on CPU CrocSort and GPU CrocSort, comparing all SortStats metrics.

ideas.title: Streaming GPU CrocSort for Continuous Data Ingestion
ideas.summary: Extend GPU CrocSort for streaming sort: continuously ingest data, maintain sorted runs in HBM, and merge on-demand.
ideas.crocsort_ref: src/replacement_selection/mod.rs:ReplacementScanner — streaming input interface; RunSink streaming output
ideas.crocsort_concept: Replacement selection: streaming algorithm that ingests records continuously and emits sorted runs
ideas.gpu_primitive: CUDA streams for concurrent ingest+sort, persistent kernel for continuous operation
ideas.detail: CrocSort's replacement selection is inherently streaming — it accepts records one at a time and emits sorted runs. On GPU, a persistent kernel continuously runs replacement selection: new data arrives via PCIe/NVLink to a ring buffer, the kernel ingests records and generates runs to HBM. Merge is triggered when run count exceeds a threshold (Huffman schedule). This enables GPU-accelerated continuous sorting for real-time data pipelines.
ideas.novelty: No GPU sort supports streaming operation. CrocSort's streaming replacement selection on GPU for continuous ingestion is novel.
related_work: Chandramouli et al., Trill: High-Performance Streaming Analytics, VLDB 2015
NVIDIA, GPU Direct RDMA, 2023
ideas.experiment: Measure sustained sort throughput under continuous ingestion at 1/10/100 GB/s input rates on A100.
